text: DENIS-DE-JOUHET (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Aigurande, 8 m. SW of La Chatre. Pop. 1,832.
DENIS-DE-LILLEBONNE (SAINT), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine Inferieure, cant. and com. of Lillebonne. Pop. 1,403.
DENIS-SUR-LOIRE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. and 4 m. NE of Blois, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 522.
DENIS - D'ORQUES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loué, 28 m. W of Le Mans. Pop. 2,340.
DENIS - DE - PILLE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Guitres. 7 m. N of Libourne, on the l. bank of the river Isle. Pop. 2,525.
DENIS-LEX-REBAIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Rebais. Pop. 1,102.
DENIS-SUR-SARTHON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, and 7 m. W of Alencon, on the l. bank of the Sarthon. Pop. 1,416. It has several blast-furnaces and forges.
DENIS-EN-VAL (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. and 4 m. from Orleans. Pop. 979. It has manufactories of sugar from beet-root, and produces good wine.
DENIS-LE-VETU (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Arisy-la-Salle, 4 m. S of Coutances. Pop. 464.
DENIS-DE-VILLENETTE (SAINT), a village of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Juvigny, 81 m. SE of Domfront. Pop. 438.

DENIZLU, or DEGNIZLU, a town of Asia Minor, in Anatolia, in the sanj. and 135 m. SW of Kutahiyah, on an affluent of the Kiuk - Bunnar-Su-the ancient Lycus—and at the NE extremity of the Baba- Dagh. It stands in the midst of vineyards, in a fertile plain, surrounded on all sides by mountains. The old town of D., which extended to a rising ground on the S of the present town, was destroyed by an earthquake early in the 18th cent., when 12,000 persons perished.
DENJIKOVO, a circle and village in the Russian dan, in the territory of the Shilluks, extending along the N side of the Jebel-el-Kumri, and comprising part of the upper basin of the Bahr-el-Abiad.
DENKENDORF, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 4 m. S of Esslingen, on the r. bank of the Kersch. Pop. 1,429. It has an ancient monastery.
DENKOW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomirz, 10 m. N of Opatow, and 38 m. SSE of Radom, on the l. bank of the Kamienna. Pop. 760.

DENMARK. Or DANMARK, the DEENEMERKEN of the Dutch, Danimarca of the Italians, Dannemark of the Swedes, and DANEMARK of the Germans, a secondary kingdom of Northern Europe, consisting, according to the ancient Danish divisions, of the Danish islands, and the peninsula of North Jutland on the continent of Europe. Since the dissolution of the German empire, the three duchies of Sleswick, Holstein, and Lauenburg, have been added to this kingdom; Iceland, part of Greenland, and the Faroe islands have for many ages been considered Danish settlements. Boundaries.] The kingdom of D.—confining the term to the Danish islands, the peninsula of Jutland, and the three duchies-lies on the N edge of the temperate zone, between the parallels of 53° 22' 42", and 57° 44′ 52" N, measured from the extreme S point of Lauenburg, to the N point of Jutland; and between the meridians of 25° 44' 27", and 30° 27' 02" E of Ferro, according to Baggesen, measuring from Blaavandshuk on the W coast of Jutland, to the E coast of the small island of Saltholm in the Oresunde; or between 4° 30' 20" W, and 0' 12' 15" E of the meridian of Copenhagen. The length of the mainland portion of D. measured along the meridian of 10° E long, of Greenwich, or from Hamburgh to the NW point of Jutland, is 240 geog. m.; but this line does not lie wholly on the main. From the mouth of the Elbe, to the Skagen or NE point of Jutland, a line running NNE and SSW, and wholly on the main, measures 236 geog. m. The greatest breadth on the mainland occurs between the W and E coasts of Jutland, under the parallel of 56° 27', viz. 94 geog. m. - The Sound divides Zealand or Sieland, from Sweden; the Great Belt, Funen from Sieland; the Small Belt, Funen from Sleswick on the main. Laaland, Falstar, Langeland, Alsen, and Möen, are situated in the Baltic; Samsöa, and Laessoa, in the Cattegat. The continental portion of D. forms a long projecting peninsula, washed on the W and N by the German ocean; on the NE by the Cattegat; and on the SE by the Baltic and its arms. On the S the Elbe forms the boundary with Hanover; but the territory of Hamburgh lies on the Danish side of that river. On the SE D. borders upon Mecklenburg and Lubeck. Extent and Population.] The surface of the peninsula of North Jutland and the islands, or of D. Proper, according to the most recent calculations, extends to 14,521.6 sq. m.; and that of the three duchies to 7,162.4 sq. m. The area of the whole kingdom may therefore be estimated at 21,684 sq. m. The superficial area of the different civil divisions of the kingdom is thus estimated by Baggesen: I. DENMARK PROPER: viz. Area in Pop. in 1840. 1. The Islands comprised in the Sie- sq. m. land and the Funen group, . 4,996-2 1. The Faröe Islands, 2. Iceland, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . IV. FOREIGN POSSESSIONS: viz. . 42,476 8,000 in 1836. 1. Greenland, . 2. West India islands, viz. St. Croix, St. Thomas, St. John, . . . 175 2 45,000 3. Nicobar islands, 2654 *4. Settlements on the Guinea coast, sold to Great Britain in 1849. *5. Settlements in Hindostan, viz. Tranquebar and Serampore, sold to the East India company in 1845. Physical features.] The appearance of D., particularly of the islands of Sieland and Funen, and of Sleswick upon the continent, is that of a level country in general well-cultivated. It affords nothing which can properly be called a mountain; and few eminences which in many countries would be deemed a hill,—the most elevated point rising only 1,200 ft. above the sea; but the general aspect is pleasant and cheerful, consisting of plains intersected by gentle hills, the heights for the most part clothed with pasture or shaded by tufts of trees, and numerous streams, lakes, and rivulets giving variety and animation to the scene. The coasts are in some parts steep and bold; but, in general, they are low and sandy. In the isles there are some woods, and forests occur in Jutland. Sieland is a fertile and pleasant country, with fields separated by mud walls, cottages either of brick or white-washed, woods of beech and oak, numerous small lakes, and gentle hills. Funen is equally agreeable; and is said to be as well-cultivated as most of the counties in England. Toward the NW, where the Jutland peninsula terminates in the Baltic, every thing assumes an aspect of barrenness and desolation. This region has been compared to Arabia without its rivers or its verdant oases, but not without its tempests and sands, which sometimes overwhelm here what little feeble agriculture may have cultivated, and convert the habitual penury of the Jutlander into severe and cruel misfortune. For the purpose of consolidating these sands, various kinds of trees and shrubs are planted, to destroy which is prohibited under a severe penalty. They sow also in these districts the Elymus arenarius, whose spreading roots bind the sand, while its large leaves break the force of the wind. The Arundo arenaria, and the Carex arenaria, have likewise been cultivated with the view of arresting the progress of the sand-flood in N. Jutland. The Danish continent may be described as a vast plain, through which a sandy barren ridge stretches from S to N, commencing in the Lausitzer-gebirg, running through the German counties of Mecklenburg and Lauenburg, and terminating in the extreme N in the promontory of Skagen. The highest point in this chain, the Hommelberg, does not exceed 1,000 ft. in alt. above sea-level. The coast of this peninsula is quite flat towards the German ocean, but is low, and intermixed, as we have said, with quicksands, and towards the Elbe is protected by large dams against the encroachments of the sea. Towards the Baltic, the land is more elevated, and offers finer and more picturesque points of view than the W coast, which has no wood. The German ocean has frequently burst through the isthmus which connects the N part of Jutland with the rest of the peninsula. In 1826, three rapid currents united the North sea with the Lym-fiord; similar devastations have repeatedly occurred along the W coast. Coasts and fiords.] The coasts of D. are indented by numerous branches of the sea, called fiords, or riig, which, in a commercial country, would be accounted very advantageous. Among these, by far the most considerable is the Lym-fiord, which deserves the appellation of an inland sea. It crosses the N part of the peninsula of Jutland, from W to E, and joins that part of the entrance to the Baltic which is called the Cattegat, or Skagerack, a little to the S of the 57th parallel. It expands into several extensive bays, contains many islands, and at a very small expense might be made to communicate on the W with the German ocean. Other considerable firths are the Mariager-fiord, the Horsens-fiord, the Viele-fiord, the Kolding-fiord, the Apenraderfiord, the Flensburg-fiord, the Schley, and the Eckernforder-hafen,-all inlets of the Baltic; and the Nessum-fiord, and Ringkiobing-fiord, inlets of the three straits which connect the Cattegat and Baltic, does not exceed 1,335 faths. in breadth between Helsingoer and Helsingborg. Its depth is from 10 to 19 faths.; but close upon the coast, and round some of the islands, it is only 4 faths. The Great Belt between Sieland and Funen is about 9 m. broad opposite Wyburg, but of very dangerous navigation on account of its varying depths and sand-banks. The Little Belt is 40 m. long, but not above 2 m. broad opposite Middelfahrt; and of dangerous navigation on account of the rapid current which runs from the Baltic into the Cattegat, and the numerous shallows. Rivers and lakes.] The rivers of D. are not large; indeed the extent of the country does not admit of great length of course in its rivers. Sieland, and the other islands, have no rivers at all, but only a few small brooks. The most considerable Danish river is the Eider, upon the N boundary of Holstein -once the Terminus imperii, and still separating Germany from Denmark Proper - which, originating near the Baltic, runs W, and after a course of about 56 m., falls into the German sea at Tunningen. - The Gudensaal has its source between Silkeborg and Halds; running from W to E, it becomes navigable at Randers, and then, turning towards the N, enters the Baltic at Udbye.-The other rivers, or more properly rivulets, are numerous; but, with the exception of the Gudenaae, Storaae, Stör, Suseae, Bardéaae, Skodborgeraae, and Odenseaae, are so inconsiderable as not to require even enumeration. The Elbe is a German stream, but touches the boundaries of D. on the S and SW. The Trave, which rises in Holstein and falls into the Baltic, is navigable; and most of the rivers afford convenient harbours and anchorage at their mouths.—The lakes of D. are numerous, being said to exceed 400; but none of them are of great extent. The most remarkable are those of Arre, Esrom, Sial, Fure, and Tiis in Sieland; the Arreskov, and Brendagaard in Funen; the Mariboe or Marienbua in Laaland; the lake of Wyburg, the Long lake, and the Garboal in Jutland; the lakes of Plön, Selent, and Westen, in Holstein; and in Lauenburg, the lake of Ratzeburg. Canals. D. has three canals. The canal of Sleswick-Holstein, or Kiel, which unites the German ocean with the Baltic, is about 16 m. long, and 10 ft. deep; it is 54 ft. broad at the bottom, and 100 ft. at the surface. This canal, which runs between the river Eyder and the gulf of Kiel, is extremely favourable to the internal commerce of D., by rendering unnecessary the long and dangerous voyage round the peninsula of Jutland. It is navigable by vessels of 150 tons burden. Its whole expense was estimated at £200,000.—The canal of Stecknitz, about 50 m. in length, and formed chiefly by the river of that name and the Delvenaae, unites the Elbe with the Baltic.—The canal of Odensee unites Odensee, the cap. of Funen, with the sea. Climate. The climate of D. Proper is in genera' humid, and subject to thick fogs, approaching pretty closely to that of Scotland, but more temperate than might be expected from the latitude. In winter, however, the cold is frequently severe, particularly in Jutland, and the navigation of the neighbouring seas is prevented by great quantities of ice. The summer is often oppressively warm. The humidity of the atmosphere is, however, particularly advantageous here, as one-half the soil consists of a light sand which would otherwise be quite sterile. The average temp. at Copenhagen is 6° of Reaumur; but the cold in winter is often severe, -so much so that the Sound has been frozen over. Upon a mean of 26 years, it rains 130 days in the year, and thunders German ocean. The Sound, or Oresund, one of the 13. The NW wind is severely felt in May and June. The summer begins with June, and ends with September; and the transition from summer to winter, and winter to summer, is rapid. The coldest months are December, January, and February; but during these there are often thaws of many days' duration. In March and April, the weather becomes milder, though it is subject to continual changes. A calm and serene sky, and an atmosphere free of vapours, are seldom enjoyed by the inhabitants of D.; but, in general, at least in the higher situations, the air is sufficiently salubrious. The heat is greater, and continues longer, in the duchies than in the rest of D.: this is particularly the case in Holstein. The influence of the sun, joined to the quality of the soil, produces in some districts of this prov. a luxuriance of vegetation which reminds us of the S of Germany; in some places the banks of the Elbe and the Trave exhibit the appearance of those of the Rhine, the Maine, and the Neckar. Upon the whole, the climate of D. appears not less propitious to human life than to the vegetable kingdom, as instances of longevity are very common throughout this kingdom. Soil.] The soil is of various qualities, but the prevailing character is the sandy. In some parts it consists of a very rich mould, called marsch, of which the component substances are marl and a bituminous matter. The soil of Sieland and Laaland is considered the strongest: it is more mixed, and admits of a more varied cultivation in Funen, Langland, and Falster. On the E coast of Jutland, the soil is rich and favourable to vegetation; and on the W coast there are extensive alluvious, which might be turned to advantage. Between the two coasts, a sandy ridge extends through the middle of the country, repugnant to cultivation, and producing only heath and some useless plants. At the N extremity of this prov., the coast is covered with a deep stratum of dry sand, which prevails particularly in the environs of Skagen, where a great extent of surface presents only the image of sterility. Similar collections of sand are met with after turning the N point of Jutland towards the W, and also on some parts of the coast of Sieland. In all the higher parts of Sleswick and Holstein, the soil is the same as in the other provs. of D.; but on the W coast, from the mouth of the Elbe nearly as far as the frontiers of Jutland, it is of a peculiar kind. That district, like a great part of Holland, owes its existence to the agency of the waters, and consists of alluvions which for ages have been insensibly collecting. These depositions of the sea, or of the rivers, which are always extremely fertile, the industry of man has seized and converted into richly cultivated fields; but to preserve this conquest requires the most active and persevering vigilance. The waters often attempt to recover their ancient dominion; and if they meet not with barriers capable of restraining them, resume their ancient rights, inundate the cultivated grounds, and in a few hours destroy the labours of many years. The most dreadful of these devastations happened in 1634, when, from the mouth of the Elbe, to the town of Reben in Jutland, the relentless element carried everywhere destruction: thousands of cattle and sheep disappeared in this inundation; above 1,500 human beings perished; and houses, mills, and whole villages were swallowed up. The art of constructing sea-banks has been brought to greater perfection in D. since that disastrous period. These bulwarks are formed of tough clay about 19 ft. high, and so broad that a carriage can easily pass along them. They are also provided with ditches, canals, and sluices for retaining the water necessary for the irrigation of the soil, and for carrying-off what is superfluous. In Willersmarch, windmills are used for drawing the water into the canals. Often 30 or 40 of these mills are to be seen in motion at the same time. These works are the object of a special police, and overseers are appointed to see that they are kept in sufficient repair. New alluvions are continually forming on this coast, which, when they have attained the requisite maturity, are enclosed by new dikes; such a piece of ground is called kog. As a very considerable outlay is required for such undertakings, government usually grants a complete freehold possession to those who accomplish them. The remains of ancient embankments, which are frequently met with in the interior of the country, seem to indicate successive extensions of the land by these means. Holstein and Sleswick are thus naturally divided into two different regions; the one consisting of those districts which have thus been reclaimed from the sea, and which occupy the W side of the peninsula; and the other comprehending the higher grounds on the E side. The former of these divisions is called Marschland, or the Marsches, and the latter Geestland. The upper stratum or soil of Marschland is composed of a rich fat earth, more or less mixed with sand; the second stratum is pure sand, sometimes mixed with gravel; the last consists of a bluish coloured clay, which, spread upon the surface, makes excellent manure. The whole of this part of the peninsula is one uniform plain, without any other elevations than the dikes; the sameness of the scene, however, disappears amidst the pleasure which the beholder derives from the general appearance of prosperity, the richness of the cultivation, and the triumph of man over an element the most formidable. Botany.] Of the Danish provinces, Sieland is the richest in plants, and contains 56 species which have not been discovered in other parts of D. This, however, is probably owing to its having been more thoroughly investigated than any of the others. A sensible difference may be observed between the floras of the NE and SW parts of this island: the division may be well shown by drawing a line from Copenhagen to Nyekiöbing. Several plants occur in the SW parts which are not found in the NE; though none have been remarked in the latter, which have not also been found in the other parts.—Jutland ranks next, having 35 plants peculiar to itself. No other of the D. provs., in so short a space, shows such a difference in natural productions as may be observed on the E and W coasts of Jutland. The former possesses a fertile soil, and a flourishing vegetation; while the latter consists of an almost entirely barren and constantly moving sand. Between the two extends that remarkable heath which stretches from the point of Skagen far into Germany. In that part of Jutland N of Randersflord, we find marks of a more northern vegetation. In Vensyssel, Cornus Suecica is abundant; in other parts of D. it is rare.—Funen has, with a few exceptions, the same flora as Sieland. The plants peculiar to it amount to 12. Laaland and Falster are of a low and clayey nature, and consequently produce several plants which are more rare on the other islands, where the soil is more sandy and not so moist. Laaland had long an article of trade in 'manna-groats'—the seed of the Poa fluitans—which were used for food in the N of Europe; it has, however, been destroyed, by the draining of the mosses, &c. The plants peculiar to these islands amount to 10. Althora officinalis and Asparagus officinalis are here near their most northern limit, and Ligusticum Scoticum near its most southern. Möen, though the smallest of these provs., has long been known for its rich flora, which it owes partly to its more southern situation, and partly to the variety of its soils, and particularly its chalkhills, on which occur an abundance of the orchidaceous plants either not found at all or but very rarely in the other parts of D. The number of plants peculiar to Möen is about the same as in Laaland.—Bornholm contains several subalpine plants, which it has in common with the S and lower parts of Sweden and Norway. Carex extensa, Ulex Europæus, and Melilotus ornithopodiodes, appear to have reached their most eastern and northern limits on Bornholm. Most of the subalpine plants grow on the primitive rocks. The top of Bytterknegten, which is in this district, is said to be the highest point of D.; yet according to Oersted and Esmarck, it is not more than 500 ft. above sea-level In this island we find about as many plants as in Laaland, which are not in the other islands. Sleswick only possesses 5 species which are not found in the D. provs. or in Holstein; and 12 which are not found in D., but which it has in common with Holstein and Lauenburg. In Holstein are 77 species which do not occur in the D. provs., 35 of which are not in the other duchies. The reason why Holstein is so much richer than Sleswick, may be partly from its freer communication with the Continent by means of the Elbe, and partly from its more southern situation; for as to soil, &c., they are much alike.-Lauenburg, skirting with Mecklenburg, shows an extraordinary anomaly in its possessing plants belonging to a much more northern vegetation, as Lolium palustre, Linnæa borealis, Pedicularis sceptrum, Cinclidium stygiam, &c. It possesses 104 species which are not found in the D. provs., and 66 of which do not occur in the other duchies. The D. provs.—exclusive of Iceland, Faroe, and Greenland—contain, as far as is known, 1,197 species of monocotyledonous and dicotyledonous, or perfect plants. Of these 322 belong to the monocotyledonous, or a few more than 1. The dicotyledonous are 2 of the whole number; the same proportion that Humboldt gives for the temperate zone. [Professor Horneman in 1821.] Animal kingdom.] Denmark produces horses of a very good breed, particularly those of the district of Anglen; and remarkably fine cattle. Sheep and swine are common; goats and asses are rare. The large Danish dog is of a particular species, and celebrated for its strength and fidelity. The forests are frequented by stags, deer, roes, hares, and a variety of game; but bears are no longer found in D., and the wolf is nearly extinct. Wild fowls are numerous. Among these, the eider duck deserves particular notice, on account of its valuable down. The seas abound in fish. In both the Belts are caught small but very fat herrings, which, salted and smoked, are exported in large quantities to Germany. The fisheries of the Lymfiord are highly important. Many lakes, rivers, and artificial ponds, abound in excellent eels, trout, pike, and lamprey. In Holstein, the landholder will sometimes draw fish to the value of from £50 to £150 annually, from his fish ponds. There are banks of oysters near Skagen, and on the W coast of Jutland and Sleswick. Mineralogy. The basis or fundamental rock of Jutland, Sleswick, Holstein, Sieland, Laaland, Falster, &c., appears to be sandstone. On it rests chalk, which varies in hardness, contains flint and numerous petrifactions, and is covered with or contains beds of gypsum, which sometimes forms considerable eminences, as at Segeberg near Kiel. The most striking sections are on the coast; and of these the most remarkable is that of Stevensklint in Sieland. Here, immediately over the chalk, rests a remarkable bed of marl from a few feet to upwards of 70 ft. thick, and containing fragments of chalk, and abundance of loose masses of flint. It is covered with a bed of loam; there is an uninterrupted transition from the loam into the subjacent marl; and both of these beds appear to have been formed from the chalk. Superimposed on the loam is a vast bed of sand, which is sometimes in the state of blowing sand, sometimes more or less aggregated together, by means of clay or marl, so as to form a kind of sandstone. In some places we observe beds of this sandstone alternating with a peat which is uncommonly like coal. The sand is covered or intermixed with boulders, or rolled stones of different kinds, as of granite, gneiss, sienite, porphyry, &c., which, from their nature, and their connection with the subjacent alluvial matters, appear to have been transported from the mountains and hills of Scandinavia and Germany. The sand is more or less extensively and deeply covered with peat, which is the principal fuel of these countries; and sometimes the peat is associated with bog-iron ore.—The minerals of D., in an economical point of view, are not of very great importance. Coal, that most useful mineral, occurs but in small quantity, and, as far as we yet know, only in the island of Bornhohn. Peat, as already remarked, is the principal kind of fuel; and some varieties of it are so bituminous that the peasants use it instead of candles. Amber is found, along with brown coal, in the island of Bornholm, and also floating on the coasts of Sieland, and other islands in the Baltic. The island of Bornholm also furnishes good porcelainearth, excellent building-stones, small but beautifully transparent rock-crystals, and a kind of limestone of the nature of marble. Near Kiel in Holstein are gypsum-quarries, which have been worked for a long series of years. Those of Segeberg, which belong to the Crown, employed about 70 individuals, and from 1773 to 1793 yielded a profit of 1,192,351 dollars. The salt springs in the neighbourhood of Oldesloe have been known since the 12th cent. The water is raised by means of mills and pumps into buildings prepared to receive it, and the process here is the same as takes place in other salt-works of the same nature. The number of workmen formerly employed here was about 40, and the quantity of salt produced averaged 18,000 tons annually, which was more than sufficient for the consumption of the country. The inhabitants of Jutland procure a small quantity of salt from sea-weed, after burning it to ashes. Agriculture.] Nature has not particularly favoured D. either in fertility of soil, benignity of climate, or in the spirit and industry of its inhabitants. There are, however, many districts which might be rendered extremely fruitful, and almost none which are wholly unsusceptible of culture. The system of agricultural operations pursued in D. is pretty nearly the same as that of the N of Germany, though not conducted with the same skill. Since the abolishment of servitude, agriculture has made considerable progress in D., but there remain many traces of the feudal system, and agriculture has still to struggle with many difficulties. The prov. of Jutland in some years furnishes a small surplus above the internal consumption of buck-wheat, and, more rarely, of rye. Holstein and Lauenburg are the best cultivated districts; and it is from them that the exportation of wheat and oats chiefly takes place. The grain principally cultivated in Sleswick is oats. Laaland and Falster are indeed fertile and productive for their extent in wheat, pease, and beans; but there, as well as in the beautiful plains of Sieland and Funen, agricultural skill is little known. Sleswick has a good soil on the E and W coast; but a great portion of its soil is barren heath; and Jutland is behind all the other provs., except in the rearing of cattle. The grains chiefly cultivated are wheat, rye, barley, oats, buck-wheat, and pease. The total quantity of corn and flour exported in 1839 was 2,186,557 tönde or barrels, equal to 1,093,278 imperial quarters English. In 1847, the export was 1,310,000 quarters, of which 859,682 quarters were from D. Proper.—The rearing of cattle is, upon the whole, conducted with more skill than the raising of corn. In 1839, 11,674 horses, 27,904 oxen, and 16,264 sheep and lambs were exported from D. In 1847, 11,250 horses valued at £11 2s. 6d. per head; 44,084 head of cattle valued at from £3 5s. to £7 10s. per head; 15,186 calves valued at 15s. per head; 15,749 swine valued at 22s. 6d. per head; and 23,603 sheep and lambs averaged at 7s. 6d. per head, were exported. The export of wool in 1847 was 23,951 cwts.; of flax and hemp, 215,674 lbs. Each peasant cultivates hemp and flax for domestic use. A great quantity of butter and cheese is manufactured; and both are very largely exported from the duchies, especially Holstein. The export of cheese in 1847 was 8,770 cwts. About nine-tenths of the lands in D. Proper are cultivated by peasants, who generally possess an estate of 50 to 60 tonnes each. The Danish tonne or acre is an area of 14,000 sq. ells of 2 ft., or about 9,333 English sq. yds. The estate of a lord of a manor is either cultivated by himself, or let out in farms of different extent, leased to tenants for their life-time, and that of their wives. When the lands belonging to the manor are let-which is often the case—the situation of the persons who lease them may in some measure be compared with that of the English farmers. These lands are let for a certain number of years, in general 12, during which time the farmer is obliged to cultivate the lands after a certain plan mentioned in the lease; to pay an annual rent in money or in grain, or partly in money and partly in grain; as also to pay the taxes, and to take upon himself all the duties annexed to the tenancy. But these being comprised in the number of the privileged estates, the former is free from certain duties imposed by government on the unprivileged estates, such as furnishing horses for royal journies, working on the roads, &c., &c.; but he is obliged to pay the poor tax. The farms belonging to the manor are let to the tenants during their own life-time, and that of their wives. It might be supposed that the certainty of keeping these farms in undisturbed possession during the lifetime of husband and wife, would induce the tenants to improve their lands as much as lay in their power; but want of skill and capital, much of their time being lost in the service of the lord of the manor, and in carrying tithes to him, and the parson (in cases where they are paid by produce), and finally, the apprehension that should they make improvements the rent would be raised in proportion if their sons should get the land after them, are causes which will always occasion such lands to remain in an unimproved state of cultivation. The estates of the second order have their origin in the partition of the estates of the great landed proprietors, and the sales of the farms belonging to them. These estates, which in general are from 200 to 500 tonnes of land, and in the possession of gentlemen, are usually cultivated by the proprietors, who may be compared to the gentlemen-farmers in England. These lands are the best cultivated in D., as the proprietors generally possess skill and industry, as well as the means of improving them; and as they are conscious their children will profit by their labours, they do not work merely for the present moment. The number, however, is not considerable. The freeholders are peasants who originally lived on the estates of the Crown, and to whom the king offered their farms, on the condition of their paying a certain fixed sum, which generally remained as first mortgage on the farm, of which they had only to pay the interest. This example was followed by several gentlemen and noblemen, particularly at the commencement of the French revolution; and by that means arose a considerable number of freeholders, who possessed farms of from 50 to 60 tonnes, invested with the full rights of proprietorship, and exempt from any service to the lord. The king had hoped that these peasants, who thus reaped the whole profit of their labour, and whose possession devolved on their children, might be excited to industry and activity; but experience has proved, that wanting skill, and often capital, they do not thrive better than the tenants. The glebe lands annexed to the parsonages may be considered as similarly circumstanced. - A part of the lands of D. are cultivated by cottagers, or undertenants. Their portions of land consist generally of from 3 to 5 tonnes.-An estate of 500 tonnes, or 600 British acres, has generally from 350 to 400 tonnes of arable land, and from 100 to 150 tonnes of meadow-land. The price of such an estate, on the supposition that the land is of a tolerably good quality, with good buildings and sufficient stock, would, in the E of Jutland, be about 24,000 dollars silver; in the W of the same prov. 15,000; in Laaland from 50,000 to 70,000; and in Sieland 30,000. Considerable forests of course enhance the value, in proportion as vast heaths lessen it. The rent of such an estate in the E part of Jutland, where there is no duty-work to the lord, is about 300 quarters of barley; in the W part from 225 to 250; in Laaland from 600 to 650; and in Sieland about 300 quarters. This is paid either in produce or money, after the average price of grain fixed every year, and for every prov., by the bishop and chief magistrate of the district. The estate must be delivered over to the farmer with a sufficient supply of stock and implements, and with suitable buildings, the outside of which is kept in repair by the proprietor, and the inside by the occupying tenant. The farmer is bound to pay the taxes and duties, which upon the supposed extent and quality of estate amount to about 600 dollars. They are generally let for 12 years, and the rent paid twice annually. These farms are of different extents, but generally from 300 to 500 tonnes. Those that are above 500, or less than 250 or 300 tennes, are extremely rare. [Official Report in 1828.]-A man with a wife and four children may in D. earn 12s. a-week. The labourer's food consists of rye, oatmeal, potatoes, coffee, butter, cheese, and milk. Provisions are cheap, and a family, where economy is observed, finds in the ordinary wages of labour a sufficient provision for comfortable subsistence. Manufactures.] D. is essentially an agricultural country; its manufactures are neither numerous nor important. Government has made frequent attempts to extend them; but by aiming rather at the introduction of foreign and new manufactures, than at the encouragement of those suited to the country, and more especially by interfering too much with individual enterprise, well-meant endeavours have not been crowned with all the success that was expected. It is evident, from the constitution of the Danish corporations, that the knowledge of the mechanical arts, and almost all the different trades, have been introduced into this country by Germans. Indeed, the greater part of the tradesmen employed in all the N states of Europe came originally from Lubeck, Hamburg, Bremen, and the adjacent countries; and we still meet with numbers of German artisans in Copenhagen and other Danish towns. The vassalage of the peasants, who could exercise no trade but by the permission of their lords, and the system of corporation monopolies, were most unfavourable to the increase and prosperity of the manufacturing and mechanical arts; but recent enactments have abolished or considerably diminished the evils of these systems. It is in Copenhagen, of course, that the greatest encouragement has been given to manufactures; and the chief manufactories of D. are in that city and its environs, and in the free towns of Holstein and Sleswick. The peasantry spin and weave their own linens and woollens; but a few hands are employed at Copenhagen and Altona in the manufacture of woollen stuffs, cottons, and linens. Excellent leather is made; and the gloves of Randers and Odensee are exported in considerable quantities. By a late return, the number of sugar-refineries in the D. dominions was 46; that of paper-mills 32; ironfoundries only 4. At Frederickswerk and Hellebeck fire-arms and cannons are manufactured; coarse earthenware is made in various places; distilleries, breweries, and flour-mills are thickly scattered over the kingdom. Commerce.] No country can be better adapted for internal commerce than D. The seas with which it is encircled and intersected, and its numerous gulfs which often penetrate into the very heart of the country, and sometimes extend nearly from one sea to another, afford the utmost facility of communication between the different provinces. In the long dark foggy nights of winter, the Cattegat is indeed one of the most dangerous seas in Europe, and the difficulties of seamen are often increased here by the ice, which interrupts the navigation for about four months annually. But on the other hand, nature has given to D. an almost exclusive monopoly of commerce with Sweden, Norway, and the Baltic provinces,-countries in which it can dispose of nearly all its surplus grain. The interior commerce is chiefly maintained by small coasting-vessels; Holstein and Lauenburg possessing the only considerable traffic by land. These natural facilities of interior navigation are considerably lessened, however, by neglecting to clear the entrances into the harbours, which are in general much obstructed with sand and mud that has been allowed to collect for a great period of time. D. is by no means distinguished by the same facility of intercourse by land. The roads of this country long continued in the most deplorable condition, and occasioned much surprise and inconvenience to travellers. Public attention was at length directed to this subject, and in 1778, a plan for the general improvement of the roads throughout the country was adopted, and each proprietor assessed in an annual portion of labour or money for this purpose. This plan has been gradually executing, and there are now some roads in D., particularly that from Elsineur by Copenhagen to Corsoer on the shores of the Great Belt, equal to those of any other country. The principal internal traffic of D. is carried on in the markets and fairs, which are held in the different cities, at which the manufactures and commodities of the place, and the productions of the neighbouring country, are exposed to sale. Copenhagen, Odensee, Aalborg, Ripera, Fredericia, and Aarhuus, enjoy the principal commercial traffic of D. Proper. Several navigable rivers, the Elbe, the Trave, the Stoer, and the Eyder, facilitate the interior communication of the duchies. Altona, Flensburg, Kiel, Husum, Christiansfeld, and Toendern, have the principal share of the commerce of these provs. "The pop. of the duchies was estimated in 1840 at 804,000; say for Sleswick 349,000; for Holstein 455,000; or nearly two-fifths of the total pop. of the Danish dominions in Europe, the duchies included, which at the same date was calculated at 2,087,000. It was not altogether to the development intrinsically of commerce that the rapid and extraordinary multiplication of exchanges was attributable. Circumstances apart materially assisted. Thus by legislative and administrative modifications in 1838 the tariff of duties, previously exorbitant both on imports and exports, was revised and reduced; contraband trade was in consequence put down. Taxation by weight and by categories was substituted for taxes ad valorem. Again: those privileges were abolished in virtue of which the proprietors of lands noble of the duchies were exempt from customs duties. From thence, augmentation of the number of tax-payers, and a more equal repartition of public burdens. This abolition of privileges by the government, in 1838, is probably not among the least important of the causes which, since that period, have involved the duchies in so much political agitation, and excited so much aversion to the rule of D. The nobles and the holders of lands noble were aggrieved by what they would regard as a spoliation of their ancient feudal rights. [Daily News.]-The trade to Iceland was long in the hands of successive companies of Danish merchants, who ruined the province in their short-sighted efforts to enrich themselves. In 1789, a free trade with this island was allowed to all the inhabitants of the D. states, but foreigners are still extirely excluded from it. The imports from Iceland, which consist of dried and salted fish, oil, salted beef and mutton, tallow, hides, feathers, eider-down, sulphur, wool, stockings, mittens, &c., amount annually to 200,000 rix-dollars. The commodities sent to that island amount to about 150,000 rix-d., consisting chiefly of meal, beer, spirituous liquors, wine, woollen and linen cloths, hats, coffee, tea, spices, salt, ironmongery, paper, and soap. Since 1787, Bergen, Christiansand, Altona, and some other places, have shared with the cap. the profits of the trade to Iceland. A packetboat sails in spring from Copenhagen to Iceland, and returns in autumn.-D. imports from the Faroe islands—the trade of which has not yet been declared free-dried and salted fish, oil, feathers, hides, tallow, and woollen stockings; and sends thither meal, spirituous liquors, tea, coffee, sugar, spices, linen, glass, and several other articles. The exports to these islands amount at an average to 22,251 rix-d. yearly, and the imports to nearly the same sum.—D. sends out a considerable number of vessels to the whalefisheries of Greenland. It imports besides from the settlements on the Greenland coast, oil, fish, eiderdown, and furs; and sends out meal, coarse woollen cloths, tobacco, spirituous liquors, sugar, and spices. The exports to Greenland will amount to about 80,000 rix-d. yearly; and the imports, including the produce of the whale-fisheries - which is fully the half-to about 180,000 rix-d.-The commerce of D. with its islands, like that with its other distant possessions, was long carried on by oppressive monopolies. Before, however, the war with Britain suspended this branch of commerce, by depriving them of their islands, the trade to St. John and St. Thomas had become free to all the subjects of D. Vessels were likewise sent from all the D. ports to the island of St. Croix; but the cargoes they brought home were to be delivered at Copenhagen, unless the ships belonged to some of those cities in which sugar-houses had been established. The cargoes destined for these possessions consisted of laces of all kinds, iron, copper, Danish and foreign manufactures, and Indian commodities. Sugar, rum, and cotton, were the principal returns of the islands; but indigo, tobacco, mahogany, and coffee, were also procured from other places through the medium of these colonies. The number of ships employed in this branch of Danish commerce varied according to circumstances; but they may have amounted at an average to between 70 and 80, of from 80 to 120 tons burden. The slave trade was long with the Danes, as with the other commercial states of Europe, the principal branch of commerce on the coast of Africa.-In 1616, a Danish East India company was established by Christian IV. This, as also two other successive companies, has been dissolved. The present East India or Asiatic company was established in 1732, with the exclusive right of trading to all places between the Cape of Good Hope and China for 40 years. When the charter expired in 1772, it was renewed for 20 years, but with considerable alterations. The commerce with China was again bestowed exclusively on the company, but that with India was opened to individuals on certain conditions. In 1792, the company obtained a second renewal of their charter for other 20 years, on nearly the same terms as the former; but the freedom of the Indian trade was then still further extended. Vessels may now be freighted by private adventurers, not only in all the Danish, but also in foreign ports, on obtaining the necessary licenses: their cargoes on their return must however be brought to Copenhagen, and sold by auction. Tea, rhubarb, and porcelain, are the principal articles brought from China. The establishments in India—also recently renounced -furnished calicoes, silks, sugar, rice, pepper, ginger, cinnamon, indigo, opium, arrack, and saffron. Besides silver for China, D. ships for the Indian market metals, spirituous liquors, pitch, and various manufactures.-The merchants of D. have formed relations with most commercial states. They frequent the ports of the Baltic, particularly St. Petersburg, Riga, and Memel, with herring, dried fish, the woollen manufactures of Iceland and the Faroe islands, salt from France, Spain, and Portugal, the commodities of India and China, oysters, and dog-skin gloves; and they bring home potashes, planks, firewood, hemp, flax, cordage, iron, copper, and linen. The commerce with Germany is important. The Germans draw from this country a great number of horses, cattle fattened in the pastures of Marscheland, butter, cheese, fish, oil, woollen stockings, sugar, and tea; and give in exchange thread, linen, wool, brandy, wines, ironmongery, paper, and books. The inhabitants of Sleswick and Holstein have long maintained an intimate commercial intercourse with the Dutch, from whom there is great demand for rapeseed, wood, fish, &c.; and who give in return spices, medicines, seeds, tobacco-pipes, and paper. Most of the commercial towns of France are well acquainted with the Danish flag. The articles of exchange are on the one side butter, cheese, horses, fish, wood, and various Russian commodities; and on the other salt, wines, fruits, brandy, silk and woollen stuffs, and paper. D. exports to Spain and Portugal nearly the same articles as to France; and receives in return advantages which she had formerly enjoyed in her relations with Norway. The trade with that country in grain, provisions, and colonial produce, was exclusively in the hands of the Danes; the merchants of Copenhagen were in fact the bankers of Norway, and as such controlled nearly the whole of her commerce, not only that which related to D., but also the trade with the rest of Europe: the exchange at Copenhagen was the mart where most of the commercial affairs of Norway were arranged. The simple interchange of their superfluous produce now forms the trade between the two countries. Grain, the demand for which fluctuates of course with the character of the Norwegian harvests, forms the most important article of export to Norway. With the exception of about 6,000 Jews, the population of D. is entirely of Teutonic derivation. There are three branches of the original stem in this country: viz. 1st. Danes, occupying the Islands, Jutland, and a small part of Sleswick, and speaking a language undoubtedly a dialect of the German, and of easy acquisition—except as regards pronunciation—either to a Dutchman, a German, or an Englishman. The Danes have regular and well-formed features, frequently fair or brownish hair, blue eyes, and a body capable of sustaining great fatigue. The women are of a more delicate frame. Their complexion is dazzlingly white; but, upon the whole, their features are destitute of animation, and soon after 25 they begin to lose their charms. The Dane is gallant and brave, a spirited sailor or soldier, but averse to enterprise. A German authority tells us, the Dane has less cordiality and confidence than the German, but more obstinacy; he is an acute observer, a calm reasoner, and frequently gifted with a vivid conception. The Danes usually follow the French fashions; and the language of that nation is very generally spoken in D. 2d. Germans, chiefly settled in Holstein, Lubeck, and Sleswick; their language is also predominant as far as the boundaries of Jutland, and is much used in company. The aim of the learned professors of Kiel, and some other leading Germans, has long been to attach the S or continental part of D. to the German empire. In this, they have in a great measure succeeded with Holstein, in whose pop. the Germanic element undoubtedly predominates, but have hitherto failed with respect to Schleswig. The case of this intermediate duchy is thus stated by the Chevalier Bunsen, on the German side, and a Sleswicker on the other. "The duchy of Schleswig, or the country of the Angles, situated between Jutland and Holstein," says the Chevalier, "has about 700,000 inhabitants, amongst whom there are about 150,000 peasants of Danish origin, immigrated from the bordering districts of Jutland, almost all of whom live in the N parts of Schleswig, and some along the middle ridge of the country down to Glucksburg. The rest are Germans; and the civilizing element, in schools, and by the Bible and worship, has always been, and is at present, German. This circumstance, and the common love of liberty, explain the phenomenon, that the Danish pop. of the duchy has always united with the German in the assertion and support of the independence of the duchy." In all this, responds the Danish-Sleswicker, there is scarcely one word true. "The facts of the case are: Schleswig contains 165 Danish sq. m., of which the 100 sq. m. are inhabited by Danes, which corresponds at least much better with the chevalier's own statement that a line behind Glucksburg, Flensborg, and Tondern, would be the line between the Danish Schleswig and the German Schleswig, than his statement that the Danes should form no greater part of the pop. than 150,000 out of 700,000. But Schleswig has not 700,000 inhabitants; after the last tables of pop. it has 362,000, of whom 190,000 are Danes, and only 147,000 real Germans; for the remaining 25,000 Frizes—who not only, as Bunsen relates, inhabit the W islands, but indeed the whole W marsh coast—do not side at all with the insurgents. The present provisional government in Rendsburg tried to stir them and induce them to occupy Tondern, from which the inhabitants, with assistance of the peasants, had driven away their German revolutionary functionaries; but they refused, and declared their intention to be faithful to their king. The chevalier relates that the Danish inhabitants of Schleswig have immigrated from Jutland. This is not the case: immigration never took place from the kingdom to the duchy; on the contrary, Schleswigers have always immigrated to Jutland, the land there being cheaper and less cultivated, except that Jutes, as servants, have occasionally immigrated to the land of the Frizes, but generally again returned. The fact is, that Schleswig or S. Jutland was in elder times quite a Danish prov., as Gruner declares. Before the prov. was named Schleswig, it is known that the Danes erected the Dannevirke or wall from the Shley to and along the Eider. The land not protected by this wall, especially the peninsula Danishwald [Danish wood], was an uninhabited border-land between the Danes and the Germans. This part was in latter times inhabited especially by Germans, but has never belonged to Holstein. The chevalier says: 'The civilizing element has always been, and is at present, German.' This is not the case. In the whole N part, for more than 120,000 inhabitants, there is no German church, no German school; in the middle part the Danish and German languages are used alternately. Certain it is that 50,000 Danish inhabitants have at present the German language in church and school; but the king, in 1840, pursuant to a petition of the Schleswig states in 1838, declared that the Danish language should be gradually restored in churches, schools, and in the courts of law, where it is the language of the people. Chevalier Bunsen seems not to have known that the states petitioned in that way. The pretender, the Duke of Augustenburg, in the debates in the chamber, spoke strongly against some members who argued in a way similar to Bunsen with respect to the Danish pop. in Schleswig; and Falck, one of the most celebrated Schleswig-Holsteiners, was an ardent promoter of this act of justice. I will, however, not conceal that the states in later years have petitioned the king to repeal that declaration, which they had themselves provoked. That 'the two duchies should have been united from time immemorial,' as the chevalier asserts, can only be true in German memories: we Schleswigers know and remember it otherwise. Bunsen tells us 'both duchies have the same law:' The fact is, Holstein has as its fundamental law the corpus juris Romani, but Schleswig has always had, and has still, 'the Jütshe law,' given in 1240 by Valdemar II., for all Jütland. It is not true, as it is generally related, that Holstein and Schleswig have revolted. The truth is that the insurrection broke out in Kiel in Holstein, and that the insurrectionary Holstein then tried to conquer Schleswig, where only a party sided with the insurgents. Only the three southern cities, Rendsburg, Eckernförde, and Schleswig, joined the insurrection actively; Haderlay, Apenrade, and Tonden drove their German functionaries away without assistance from the king. The important islands Aeroe and Alsen, with 38,000 Danish inhabitants, never joined the insurrection. In the battle at Bau the whole advanced guard of the Danish army was formed of Schleswigers." 3d. Frises and Angles, two German tribes, located on the W coast of Sleswick, and on the islands of the German ocean. It is only in the bailiwicks of Tondern and Bredstedt, and in some of the islands, that the Frisian language is spoken in its purity. The other Frises speak a kind of Low German; the Angles, a corrupted Danish. The Frises are distinguished from the other inhabitants of this kingdom by ruder manners, and a kind of national pride which looks with contempt on all other citizens of the state. They are partly agriculturists, partly sailors and fishermen. The inhabitants of the Ditmarschen in Holstein are evidently related to the Frises. The Angles-"the ancestors of the people of England as to blood and name" [Bunsen]—occupy the land between the bay of Flensburg, the Baltic, and the Schley, and amount to about 30,000. They are marked by a strong physical frame, and high notions of liberty and national honour. They are an industrious race, and distinguished by their good morals. We shall speak of the inhabitants of the Faroe islands, and of Iceland, in treating of the topography of these places. Ranks of society.] The inhabitants of D. have been divided, according to their ranks, into 5 classes: viz. 1st, The nobility, who have privileged fiefs in the kingdom, from which they derive their nobility. 2d, The titular nobility; to which class belong the companions of the two D. orders of knighthood,namely, the order of the Elephant, and the order of Dannebrog,—those counts and barons who are not possessed of counties and baronies,—and the persons filling the higher offices of the state; all of whichwhether civil, military, or ecclesiastical-confer on those who hold them a certain nobility during their lives. 3d, The inferior clergy, lawyers, and students. 4th, The merchants and citizens of great towns. And 5th, The seamen and pop. engaged in agriculture: the latter exceeding 1,000,000 in number.—The proportion of paupers in Denmark is said to be 3 per cent., which is only the fifth of that of Holland.-It has been affirmed—and, we believe, with truth—that before the government of D. was made hereditary and absolute, the nobility and gentry of that country lived in great splendour and affluence. Their country-seats were magnificent, and their hospitality unbounded; and when the States were annually assem- <i>めい アクラフラク カンプスストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストストス</i> bled, they met their sovereign with retinues as numerous and brilliant as his own. They are now said to have greatly declined in magnificence of living, and to diminish daily in number and credit: still, however, there are many families which exhibit all the elegance and refinement visible in the best European society; and amongst the nobility generally we meet with considerable remains of ancient stateliness. The food of the lower classes consists chiefly of oat-cakes, rye-bread, fish, and cheese; but the tables of the wealthier classes exhibit the usual profusion of European luxuries. Clarke-to whose keen eye we are indebted for many lively and sagacious observations on national characteristics and manners -observes that a journey from London to Copenhagen might exhibit the retrocession of a century; every thing being found, in the latter city, as it existed in the former a hundred years before,—an observation extending not only to the amusements, the dress, and the manners of the people, but to the general state of everything connected with Danish society; excepting, perhaps, the commerce of the country, which is upon an improving footing. Government.] The government of D., like that of most of the Gothic nations, was originally an elective monarchy: the right of election being vested in the three estates of the kingdom,—the nobles, the clergy, and the commons, -who exercised the supreme legislative authority by means of representatives assembled in diet, while the executive power was vested in the king, and a senate composed of the higher nobles. Such was the equable and wise constitution of D. until the year 1660, when the two inferior estates, to punish the tyranny of the nobles, renounced their most precious birthrights as citizens of a free state, and placed their liberties in the hand of an unlimited hereditary monarch! The original fundamental laws of the D. constitution are: 1st, The Act of Sovereignty [Enevolds—Arveregierings—Akten], presented on 10th Jan. 1661 to Frederic III. It consists of forty articles; of which the following are the most remarkable: "The hereditary kings of D. and Norway shall be in effect, and ought to be esteemed by their subjects, the only supreme head on earth: they shall be above all human laws, and shall acknowledge, in all ecclesiastical and civil affairs, no higher power than God alone. The king shall enjoy the right of making and interpreting the laws,—of abrogating, adding to, and dispensing with them. He may also annul all the laws which either he or his predecessors shall have made, excepting this royal law, which must remain irrevocable, and be considered as the fundamental law of the state. He has the power of declaring war, making peace, imposing taxes, and levying contributions of all sorts," &c. &c.! 2d, The king's law [Kongeloven], pronounced on 14th Nov. 1765, defining the royal prerogatives and obligations. 3d. The Act of citizenship [Ind fots retten], promulgated in 1776, by which it is declared that none but natives shall be eligible to civil or military employments under government.—In May 1834, a new organic law was promulgated, which established a new subdivision of the D. European dominions, and instituted a provincial assembly for each division. The four provs. were: The Islands, N. Jutland, S. Jutland, and the duchy of Holstein. The prov. of the Islands was allowed an assembly of 70 representatives, whose place of meeting is Roeskilde in Sieland. The prov. of N. Jutland, consisting of the whole peninsula, has an assembly of 55 members, who meet at Wyburg. The prov. of S. Jutland, composed of the duchy of Sleswick, had 44 representatives assigned to it, who assemble in Sleswick. The prov. of Holstein sends 48 representatives to its assembly at Ibzehoe. The duchy of Lauenburg had already a diet of its own, consisting of 25 members. By this constitution, the whole D. monarchy is represented in 5 different houses-of-assembly by 242 members, 28 of whom, viz. 7 in each of the 4 provincial assemblies, were nominated by the sovereign himself. The members-each of whom has a substitute regularly elected with him-are elected for 6 years, and meet every 2 years, but not without a previous summons from the sovereign. These assemblies are excluded from the right of voting taxes, or of enacting laws,-being consultive only; but any member may propose laws or modifications of laws .- On the 28th of January 1848, royal letters patent appeared ordaining that united diets of the kingdom of D., and of the duchies of Sleswick and Holstein, shall regularly at certain times, and at appointed intervals, assemble alternately in the kingdom and in the duchies, at such places as shall hereafter be named, an equal number of deputies being returned by the kingdom and by the duchies. "The rights which we shall assign to these states," this document proceeds to say, "in respect of several matters which are to be laid down in the constitutional law, shall consist in a conclusive co-operation in the alterations of the tariff, and in the administration of finance,—as also in framing laws which relate to the common interests of the kingdom and the duchies; and we shall, therefore, permit that the said states shall humbly submit to our royal consideration proposals touching such points of their operations as shall have reference to the common interests of the kingdom and the duchies. This constitution of the states-which we introduce of our own free royal will and pleasure-shall not in any way alter the regulation of the 28th of May, 1832, or the ordinance of the 15th May, 1834, respecting the establishment of provincial assemblies in our kingdom of D. and our duchies; nor the permanent union of these duchies, and the relations of our duchies of Holstein and Lauenburg to the Germanic confederation; nor the constitution of the last-named duchies; nor the establishment of the Icelandic Allhing, introduced by the ordinance of the 8th March, 1845. Lastly, the constitutional law shall contain suitable measures for the protection of the Danish, as well as of the German language, in the districts of the duchy of Sleswick. Before we confer the power of law upon the arrangements which are to be introduced into the constitutional law, they shall be submitted to the united careful consideration of enlightened and experienced men, who possess the esteem and confidence of their fellowburghers." The ordinance goes on to declare the manner in which 26 deputies for D., and the like number for the duchies, shall be chosen for the purpose of revising the new arrangements for a collective constitution. Delegates were accordingly chosen. but the revolution of March, 1848, disappointed this fair scheme. The German party, dreading the influence of the Ultra-Danish party, raised the standard of revolt; and that struggle began which is still at this moment deluging the plains of Holstein with blood. We have already touched upon what is called the Sleswick- Holstein question. The following able resumé of it is from a German journal published at Preetz in Holstein. After mentioning the royal letters patent and the events that followed its issue. this article proceeds as follows: "This Schleswig-Holstein affair is certainly a doubtful and very complicated case; and has become still more so, partially through carelessness at an earlier period. The hereditary succession of the Danish royal law, with its hereditary right of the relatives, and of the female branch, in a greater appropinquity of degree, stands incontestably firm as regards D. Proper. In Schleswig the affair is not so clear as the Schleswig-Holstein party asserts, and as our movement-men repeat. The entangled history of these countries,—manifold and conflicting interests,—strange mixture of titles of right,—obscure and self-contradictory declarations,-concessions exterted by circumstances,-imperfect and disputable documents,-here form sovariegated a mixture of the grounds of decision, that, if the matter were the subject of a private lawsuit, an occasion would be given for the most protracted and most doubtful dispute of right. Schleswig is bound to Germany by nature, and inhabited by a German tribe. But the leading tribe of its pop. was originally the same as that which inhabits the Danish islands, and, according to the right of nations, it has never been united to Germany. It emerges from the night of history as a Danish province; but is certainly separated, at an early period, as an individual principality; this, however, only on behalf of its defence against Slavonia and Saxony, and under dukes from the old Danish royal race, to whom it was awarded as a Danish military fief. After the extinction of this race, the Holstein counts,—less through certain claims of right than by means of their power and political interests,—succeeded in procuring the bestowal of Schleswig upon themselves; and since then, since the year 1386, Schleswig and Holstein have been united. Schleswig, however, always remained a Danish fief, which the counts afterwards dukes of Holstein possessed, but which was estranged from the German confederation and held under peculiar titles of right. The case became more complicated when, after the extinction of the male branch of the Holstein duke of Schleswig, the united Schleswig-Holstein states elected King Christian I., of the house of Oldenburg, as duke of Schleswig and Holstein, in 1460. That monarch, the patriarch of the present dynasty, nevertheless made an express declaration that he had been elected by the states, not as King of Denmark, but as Duke of Schleswig and Count of Holstein; that those countries should always remain together unseparated, and the states possess the right of electing their future lord out of his family. This last-right was renounced by the states at the end of the 16th cent., and the hereditary succession of the first-born substituted in its place. The remaining rights have been continually confirmed. The greatest complications, however, have been caused by the many divisions of the country, which were for the most part determined very groundlessly, and without any firm decision of the rights and boundaries, and led to vehement disputes and even wars amongst the lineal descendants. In the sequel, the King of Denmark succeeded, after the overthrow of Charles XIL of Sweden, who had protected the ducal line, in treating the ducal portion of Schleswig as an obsolete fief, and thereupon the Danish claims are principally based. At that time, however, no individual incorporation with D. followed; and, generally speaking, the circumstances of the entire duchy underwent no alteration, the government of the ducal portion having been merely conveyed to the royal line. Even on the conclusion of the treaties which at length, in 1773, brought the whole duchy of Holstein under the dominion of one sceptre, the Danish hereditary succession was by no means introduced, but, on the contrary, all the rights and franchises of the country were expressly confirmed. Lauenburg has at length become a purely German country, in which the hereditary succession of the agnates has always prevailed. But it is true that if things had been regulated according to rightful hereditary succession, Lauenburg would neither have been in Danish hands nor in the hands of those who abandoned it to Denmark. It belongs to many countries; it had at length fallen into the hands of Hanover, and was exchanged by the latter with Prussia in 1815 for East Friesland. Prussia again exchanged it with D., against the rights of the latter in Swedish Pomerania and Rugen; and it was certainly a poor indemnification to D. for the loss of Norway, although the hereditary succession of the male branch, which obtains throughout all Germany, has never been abolished even in Lauenburg. It is certain that Schleswig and Holstein have a right and a great interest to remain united. It is uncertain whether Danish or German hereditary descent prevails in Schleswig; it is possible that Lauenburg devolves upon the Danish crown. It is not comprehensible how the matter is to be settled without some composition of claims. The German confederation has only an international interest in the affair so far as it is pledged to preserve the inviolability of its territory. This, however, we would suggest to the consideration of the violent, is not affected by the decision of the question of hereditary descent (however that may be settled), so long as the possession of the country now belonging to the German confederation further allows it to remain attached thereto." It is stipulated by the fundamental laws of the constitution of D. that the king must subscribe the Augsburg confession of Faith. The crown is hereditary in the male and female line. The monarch is of age at 14; the nearest prince of the blood may become regent at 18 years of age.—As duke of Holstein and Lauenburg, the king of D. is a member of the Germanic confederation, and possesses 3 votes in plenum.—The arms of D. are the Dannebrog cross, which divides the shield into four fields, occupied by the emblems of the different provinces. The noblest order is that of the Elephant, founded in 1580. Administration.] There is no other government in Europe of which the fundamental laws authorize such an unlimited monarchy as that of D.; yet there are few states in which greater practical freedom exists. Every thing in the political and juridical constitutions is so much regulated here by forms and customs, that the Danes justly look upon these forms as forming a body of political privileges which could never be violated with impunity by the king or his ministers.—The highest tribunal of D. is the privy council; which is usually composed of the great officers of state, and the princes of the blood, with the king as president. All the higher affairs of government-with the exception of the distribution of patronage, which the king keeps in his own hand—are conducted by this council. The ministry consists of a minister-of-justice, who is aided by a Danish chancellory, and a chancellory for the duchies, and whose department embraces all matters connected with the administration of law, police, ecclesiastical affairs, and public instruction; a minister-of-finance, who likewise superintends all affairs connected with agriculture, roads and bridges, sea-dikes, royal domains and forests; a minister-of-foreign-affairs; a revenue-commission; a council-of-expenditure; a college of admiralty, and marine commissariat; a general military college and commissariat; a post-office direction, or commission; and a university direction.—D. Proper is divided into seven stiftsampters, the sees of as many bishops,— each of which is subdivided into amts, or under bailiwicks; and these last into herreders.—Sleswick and Holstein have a special governor.—Lauenburg has a special governor and government.-Iceland forms a single bailiwick with its own administration. The Faroe islands are included in the bailiwick of Sieland.—The colonies are superintended by governors. Laws and administration.] The laws of Denmark are remarkable for their equity, plainness, and brevity; the natural consequence of which is, that in this country lawsuits are rare, and the legal profession by no means lucrative. Very great advantages have II If these tribunals succeed in reconciling the parties, the agreement is registered, and it has then the same validity as the sentence of a court of law; but if they fail in this object, the proceedings are to be regarded as never having taken place; no record is made, and no expenses are incurred. The Höieste-ret, or supreme court, of which the king is president, is also the supreme military and naval court-of-appeal. The Danish chancellory has a supreme civil and criminal court-of-appeal at Copenhagen for the islands; at Wyburg, for Jutland; and at Reikevig for Iceland. The inferior magistrates for D. Proper are the herrefoged and virke-dammer, whose jurisdiction embraces a herred; the bye-foged, or town-judges; the landfoged, or territorial judges in the Faroe islands; and the sysselmands, or bailies of Iceland .-- The chancellory of the duchies comprises a court-of-appeal at Gottorf for Sleswick; at Gluckstadt for Holstein; and at Ratzburg for Lauenburg. The present code is the Dansk-lov of Christian V. founded on the code of Valdemar. There are also a code of ecclesiastical regulations and military law, and a collection of royal ordinances amounting to above 40 volumes. The duchies of Sleswick and Holstein have their own separate institutions and laws; and even several districts and towns have their own peculiar provincial laws. The Roman and Canon law, as well as the Longobard Institutions, are in use in Lauenburg. Murder is punished with death by the Danish criminal law; but robbery, housebreaking, theft, and other crimes are here punished by imprisonment or condemnation to hard labour.—The police is very strict and effective throughout the D. dominions.—Each parish forms a distinct district for the management of its own poor. The right to relief belongs to every one who is not in a condition to gain his living by work, and who, without the aid of others, would be destitute of the necessaries of life. Every person who has received relief under the poor law contracts an obligation to repay the sum received to the parish, and his chattels and the fruits of his labour are made answerable for this repayment. Begging is forbidden, and is punished with imprisonment. The parochial funds collected for relief are contributed to by the owners of land, by persons in trade, by domestic servants, and agricultural labourers-in fact, by all who are not themselves chargeable to the common fund. Revenues.] The Danish revenues are derived from customs, duties upon exports and imports, an excise on provisions and liquors, a poll-tax upon the inhabitants of D. Proper only, a tax on places under government, a land-tax, a quitrent from the royal demesnes, licenses for distilling and retailing spirits, and for permission to hunt in the royal manors, from the mines, stamped paper, and various other sources. In 1837 the total income was £1,584,133, of which £395,890 was derived from land-tax, £54,346 from house-tax, £52,543 from tax upon rank and salaries, £27,511 from tax upon property inherited, &c., £181,831 from woods and forests and other Crown property, £213,997 from the Sound toll, and £416,354 from customs and excise. The expenditure was £1,561,920, including £521,065 for interest on the public debt, and £97,882 for payments to the sinking fund. The small revenue from customs arose not from low, but from almost prohibitory duties. In 1839 the total revenue was £1,246,545. On 1st January, 1838, the public debt amounted to £13,969,035, of which £4,940,326 was contracted on a 3 per cent. stock in London.
