The expenses of the ministry of education are laid at 237,611 d.; of the ministry of justice, at 488,996 d.; ministry of the interior, 868,986 d.; foreign ministry, 255,350 d.; royal civil list, 600,000 d.; royal family, 305,416 d.; pension list, 7,322,000 d.; and the interest on the national debt, 5,264,960 d. For extraordinary and unforeseen exenses, 455,070 d. are allowed. Army.] The Danish army in 1809 amounted to 107,335 men. When reduced to a peace-establishment at the close of the war in 1815, it consisted of 17,169 infantry, 3,916 cavalry, 3,400 artillerymen, 295 engineers, and 937 horses; and its war complement was fixed at 49,301 infantry, 10,627 cavalry, 8,153 artillerymen, 144 field-guns, and 847 engineers. The cost of the peace-establishment was about £309,000 a-year. Every citizen serves for two years; and for a month in the year during four following years; he is then rated on the militia, and is only liable to emergency service. The contingent for the German confederation, from the duchies of Holstein and Lauenburg, is fixed at 3,600 men. The regular troops are chiefly composed of natives; and the quota is fixed in rather a curious manner, each district supplying one recruit for every 32 tons of corn reared in it. The period of service is 12 years.—There are two arsenals, one at Copenhagen, and one at Rendsburg .- The principal fortresses are Copenhagen, Cronenburg, Korsör, Nyburg, Friedericia, Friedrichsort, Rendsburg, Fladstrow, and Friedrichshaven.—There is an hospital of invalids at Eckernforde; and an academy for cadets-of-artillery, and another for those of the regular troops, at Copenhagen. Navy.] The Danes were formerly the most expert sailors of Europe; and D. long possessed a considerable navy, though it affords few of the materials necessary for ship-building. But the events of 1807, after the loss so lately sustained from Lord Nelson's expedition, nearly annihilated the Danish navy. In 1828, the whole Danish navy, with all the ships in the colonies, amounted only to 3 ships-of-the-line, 3 frigates, 4 sloops of 20 guns, 2 brigs, 2 schooners, 77 gun-boats, and I steam-boat. The navy at present consists of 5 sail-of-the-line, carrying in all 420 guns; 8 frigates, with 380 guns; 5 corvettes, with 106 guns; 4 brigs, with 66 guns; 3 schooners with 20 guns, and 5 cutters with 14 guns. Making a total war-fleet of 29 vessels, carrying 996 guns, besides mortar and gun boats. There were, in 1844, in the ports of Copenhagen and Elsinore, 11 steam-vessels varying from 20 to 260 tons, and of a power of from 20 to 180 horses. They are, for the most part, post-office packets, passenger-boats, and towing-steamers, plying between Kiel, Copenhagen, Callundborg, and Aarhuns, or on the Lymfiord and Aggerchannel. Most of these vessels are worked by English engineers. There were also 3 government mail steam-vessels, the largest of which does not exceed 157 tons, or measure more than 100 feet in length. Two vessels were being built at Copenhagen, to be 104 and 155 feet in length, and 20 and 25 feet in breadth, with a power of 90 and 100 horses respectively; one of these to carry two 50-pounders, and the other, two 60pounders, and four 18-pounders. The first was intended as a towing-steamer in the Sound; the second to run as a government-packet between Copenhagen and Stettin. Ecclesiastical affairs.] The government of the Lutheran church of D. appears to form a medium between the English hierarchy and the discipline of the Calvinistic church. The D. church has no archbishop; the bishops have no temporal jurisdiction; and a provost is attached to every amt who relieves the bishop from certain duties, particularly the supervision of schools. The bishop of Sieland, who has the first rank, is considered as metropolitan. The bishoprics of D. Proper and Sieland, the jurisdiction of which embraces the Faroe islands and Greenland, Laaland, Aarhuus, Aalborg, Wyburg, Fionia, and Ribe; besides these there is the bishopric of Als-et- Aeroë in Holstein; and of Skalnolt in Iceland. Holstein and Lauenburg are formed into superintendencies according to the plan of the Lutheran church. The inferior orders of clergy consist of archdeacons or provosts, to whom belong the inspection of the districts into which the different dioceses are divided; parish priests, who officiate in the parish-churches; and chaplains, who officiate in the chapels-of-ease, which are common in large parishes. The livings of the parish-priests consist of their glebe, tithes, and surplice-fees, aided in some places by voluntary donations from the people. In D. Proper, the livings of the clergy seldom exceed £400 yearly, and are seldom below £60. In Jutland, however, there are livings so low as £20 annually. In some parts, the holders of privileged lands are the patrons; in others, the parishes. There exist in Denmark several nunneries or asylums for the unmarried females of noble families, who are thus enabled at a small expense to enjoy an elegant and pleasant retirement. Schools. The means of education are within the reach of all classes in the D. states. There are a number of gymnasia or high schools in D.; and every town and village possesses a first or primary-school. All children from the age of 7 to 14 must attend some public school. The Lancasterian method was introduced under the special patronage of the king, and in 1828 had been adopted by 2,302 schools. In 1835, the elementary schools throughout the kingdom were attended by 278,500 pupils out of 300,000 between the ages of 7 and 14. Many of the schools are military, and in these gymnastic exercises have been introduced. There is a seminary at Copenhagen for instructing the teachers of the gymnasia, and 7 normal schools or pedagogical institutions throughout the kingdom. The young nobility are educated at Scröa. Besides these, there are surgical and veterinary institutions, and one or two schools for the deaf and dumb. The grammar or parish-schools are under the superintendence of a royal direction or commission, which regulates the course of study and appoints the teachers.—The university of Copenhagen, founded in 1478, has 39 professors, and about 1,000 students. That of Kiel, founded in 1665, has 31 professors, and 250 students. Literary institutions.] The royal academy of sciences at Copenhagen, founded in 1743, by Christian VI., has published many volumes of transactions. In 1763, a society was instituted for the advancement of the fine arts in D. A society for the cultivation of Northern history and Scandinavian literature was founded in 1797, and has likewise published several volumes of its labours. Another society has been instituted for the purpose of inquiring into the literature of Iceland; and a private society directs its attention to the extension of natural science. In 1809, a society was founded, under the name of Olafsen's literary society; and in 1810, a society for natural history and language was established. There are likewise the royal medical society of Copenhagen, founded in 1772; the royal agricultural society, founded in 1769; the royal society for the advancement of the veterinary art, established in 1807; a society for the advancement of national industry, founded in 1808; the patriotic society of Sleswick and Holstein, founded in 1812; and a royal society of Northern antiquities, founded in 1825. The royal library at Copenhagen contains 412,000 volumes, among which are 30,000 volumes printed previous to the date 1500, 30,000 manuscripts, and 50,000 duplicates. The library of the university at Copenhagen possesses 100,000; that of Kiel, 100,000 vols.; and both universities contain cabinets of natural history and observatories. Scandinavian languages.] The Norwegians speak and write the same language as the Danes. In Norway, however, as well as in D., this language is corrupted into various dialects among the common people, who, in both countries, retain many words belonging to the old Scandinavian or Icelandic, which are obsolete in polite conversation and writing. Yet the dialects of these languages, from the river Eyder to Finmark, scarcely present so much variety as the dialects of Germany; they gradually blend one into the other, and are not, like the German, distinctly divided into the high and the low; though in other respects, the Norwegian may be compared to the high German. Consequently there is no Norwegian language in literature; but one language common to D. and Norway, which may be termed Norwegian as well as Danish, with the exception of some variations and new introductions. The Swedes speak a somewhat different dialect, which, with regard to its inflections, more nearly resembles the ancient Scandinavian or Icelandic, but which in variety and richness differs from it the more widely, as it has adopted many foreign words, particularly from the French. The pronunciation of the Swedish more nearly resembles the German, while that of the Danish is more like the ancient Icelandic; perhaps, however, the greatest difference between the two modern Scandinavian languages consists in the orthography, which is established on very various principles. The Scandinavian nations understand each other without difficulty, and people of education can read the writings of both languages; perhaps, however, the Swedish is most difficult to the Danes, and Danish literature is more widely diffused in Sweden than the reverse. Yet any one of these languages is universally understood throughout D., Norway, Sweden, and Finland.—The territory over which the Scandinavian language prevails, extends from Sleswick in D. to St. Petersburg, and, together with the German, flows along the banks of the Neva as well as those of the Eider. The Scandinavian is spoken on the N banks and islands of the Eastern ocean, and German on the S. The northern languages differ entirely from the German, whether high or low. The difference consists in the pronunciation, as well as in the formation of the words, in the inflexions and the construction, and also in many wholly different words, though to a learned linguist the resemblance is in most cases sufficiently distinct. The Icelandic language is—as we have said—the ancient Scandinavian, whence the modern Danish and Swedish are derived. It is uniformly spoken, written, and preached throughout the whole island, and is used in all law proceedings. In D. and in Sweden no traces of the common mother-tongue remain, except a few of the oldest Runic inscriptions: yet the language of some of the old provincial laws approaches very nearly to it. In Norway there are also some written laws and other native books in the pure Icelandic language, such as it was before it became corrupted and changed. The Icelandic is a regular and cultivated language, subject to grammatical rules, similar to those of the Latin, and these rules are so accurately observed by the natives, that the most triffing blunder marks a foreigner. [Rask.] Scandinavian literature.] The literature of ancient Scandinavia comprehends the earliest literary monuments of D., Norway, Sweden, and of the islands of Faroe, Orkney, Shetland, and Iceland. This literature has been traced back to very remote antiquity, and descends to those times when the last traces of Paganism were effaced in the regions just enumerated under the footsteps of Christianity. The most remarkable circumstances attending the Scandinavian literature is, that it has not only preserved the ancient versification peculiar to all German nations, but likewise a distinct mythology, which in elegance of fancy and boldness of conception almost equals that of ancient Greece. Nor is it the versification and mythology alone which distinguish it: its invaluable historical and legislative fragments, and unique runes, open up a wide and rich field of inquiry. Julius Cæsar, Lucan, and Tacitus, notice the wild and fanciful mythology of the North; but a wide and dreary chasm occurs between the age of these historians and the next mention of Scandinavian literature. In the 8th cent., a few relics were saved from destruction by the Longobard author, Paulus Diaconus, who recites, from the traditions of his countrymen, a dialogue between Wodin and Freya. The denomination of the days of the week from the divinities and and and and and and are are are are are are are are are are Tyr, Woden, Thor, and Freya, must have been very early adopted, as they were so familiarly known in Charlemagne's time that, in giving new names to the months, that prince did not attempt to change the names of the days of the week. Nearly 300 years had elapsed when Adam of Bremen, who died in 1076, in his work De Situ Regnorum Septentrionalium, mentions a temple at Upsala erected to the gods Thor, Woden, and Freya. In the latter half of the 12th cent., Saxo Grammaticus, a Dane by birth, wrote a historical work in 16 books, entitled Historia Danica, which is a miracle of erudition for that age. Saxo's merits long remained hidden in obscurity, and a like unworthy fate was shared by three Icelanders, his contemporaries; viz., Samund Frode, who died in 1133; Are Frode, who died in 1148; and Snorro Sturleson, who was murdered in 1241. The darkness which rested upon the literature of the North, was only slowly withdrawn, even after the introduction of printing, as that invaluable art found its way into Denmark at a comparatively late period. The first printed work, viz., Exequiæ Frederici Secundi, Danai regis, bears the date of 1590. The 15th cent. witnessed the discovery of the invaluable treasures of Scandinavian literature. The parchment MS. of the prose or later Edda, written by Snorro Sturleson, was the first relic which attracted the attention of the learned of Europe. In 1628, Arngrim Johnson, a learned Icelander, forwarded to the celebrated physician Ole Worm the MS. of the later Edda, with its appendix, the Scalda, now preserved in the university library at Copenhagen. Ten years afterwards, another Icelander, Brynjulf Svenson, discovered a MS. of the poetical or earlier Edda. In these Eddas the whole Northern mythology is developed. The most ancient Edda is supposed to have been collected by Samund Sigfussom, a learned Icelandic priest, and Are Frode, the earliest historian of the North. It consists of a series of songs of the Scalds, besides a variety of ancient traditions; and it would appear that the younger, or prose Edda, was extracted from the ancient work, by Snorro Sturleson. Danish literature. Modern Danish literature has kept pace with or rather followed that of Germany, the literature of the duchies being almost exclusively German. Danish literature is confined to a very limited circle. D. has, however, produced scholars whose names would confer honour on any nation; and the number of her skilful lawyers, physicians, and divines, is not small. Philosophy has here employed several acute scholars; but the greater number have devoted themselves to the study of ancient and modern languages. The learned Danes generally speak very elegant Latin; German is almost universally understood and spoken; and even French and English are very generally known. In mathematics and astronomy, a very high rank must be given to Tycho Brahe, who stands undoubtedly at the head of Danish science. Bugge is also an eminent name in science. In history, Pontanus and Duhm are distinguished; in geography, Pontoppidan, Djurberg, Thaarup, Nyrup, Schlegel, and Malte Brun, the last of whom, though he has not written in Danish, was a Dane by birth; and in political science, several Danes have been distinguished, among whom we will only name Olafsen. Modern Danish poetry begins with popular songs and hymns; but its dawn can only be reckoned from Louis Holberg. He was followed by several esteemed poets, as Ehwald, Rahbeck, Tode, Nordahl Brun, and, above all, the spirited Jens Baggesen. Danish literature is particularly rich in dramatical works, and almost all the above-mentioned poets have also written dramas. Oelenschlager is a Dane, though his dramas are written in German. In the fine and plastic arts, D. possesses few distinguished masters, but Thorwaldson will be ranked among the first sculptors of the age, and Jual holds an eminent rank among painters. The first journal published in D. appeared in the year 1644. In 1814 there were 195 new works published in D., and 244 the preceding year, but the greater part of these works were written in German. There are at present about 80 periodical works, published daily, weekly, monthly, and quarterly in D., of which 70 are in the Danish language. The censorship is said to be liberal. Early history.] The three northern kingdoms of D., Sweden, and Norway, were anciently known by the common appellation of Scandinavia. Tacitus mentions the Suiones, or Swedes, as a seafaring people. Pliny speaks of the peninsula of Nerigon or Norway. Thule-so often mentioned by the ancients-is conjectured to have been Iceland. The name Danus first occurs in the writings of Gregory of Tours, who flourished during the 6th cent. Sweden, Norway, D, and Jutland, were inhabited in the earliest times by a German tribe, which received frequent accessions from tribes of Finnic origin. About 100 B.C. the inhabitants of Jutland—the Chersonesus Cimbrica of the Romans—and Sleswick, appear under the name of Cimbri. This people first became formidable to the Romans by the irruption which they made, in concert with the Teutones, into the beautiful province of Gallia. The Goths, under the conduct of the mysterious Odin, subsequently invaded the Scandinavian countries, and gave to D., as well as Norway and Sweden, a new dynasty of rulers. Skiold, the son of Odin, is said to have been the first king of D. His history, and that of his descendants, is so much blended with the marvellous, that this epoch is justly called the fabulous in Danish history. All that is really known of this period is, that D. was early divided into a number of petty states, composed of wild and adventurous warriors, whose principal occupation was that of piracy, and whose names carried terror through all the maritime districts of the North. When the power of the Romans began to decline, the Danes and Normans became known in the south of Europe, whither these wild sea-farers now forced their way, carrying devastation into countries which had till then been protected by Roman arms. Under the general name of Normans were comprehended, in the earliest periods of modern European history, the Danes, Swedes, and Norwegians, who founded in England two kingdoms,-established themselves under Rollo on the coast of Normandy,-peopled the Faroe islands, the Orkneys, Shetlands, Iceland, and a part of Ireland,—and finally reached Spain, Italy, and Sicily. Wherever these intrepid warriors penetrated they spread the glory of their name, but tarnished it by the fierceness of their manner and piracies. Their expeditions, however, effected little change on the national constitution of the Normans, which remained a federative system, on the plan of the German confederation, consisting of several clans or tribes, each possessing its own chief, but all paying allegiance to one king. It was not until the German kings of the Carlovingian stem began to interfere with the policy of the Normans that these tribes united more closely; but at the same time, the Norwegians and Danes separated into two distinct groups of states. Gorm subjugated Jutland in 863; and in 920 united all the small Danish states under his sceptre. With the introduction of the Christian religion by a German missionary, Ansgar from Corvey, greater order and union were established in the northern states; but the worship of Odin long struggled to maintain its place against the progress of the new religion. Sven, the grandson of Gorm, in the year 1000, conquered a part of Norway, and made an inroad into England. Canute, his more illustrious son, not only accomplished in 1016 the conquest of England, but also subdued a part of Scotland, and in 1030 conquered the whole of Norway. Under this chief the power of D. reached its highest pitch. Canute died in 1036, leaving to his successors an empire to the government of which their energies soon appeared inadequate. In 1042 England shook off its allegiance; and in a few years afterwards the example was followed by Norway. Sven Magnus Estritson established a new dynasty on the Danish throne in 1047, and revived the drooping spirits of the Danes. The wars maintained by Sven and Canute had founded the feudal system in this country, which, under the new dynasty, deprived the government of all power, and not only made the rulers dependent on the voices of the bishops and nobility, but also drained the resources of the country, reduced the peasants to a state of bondage, ruined agriculture, and threw the national commerce entirely into the hands of the German Hanseatic towns. Under this system the kings of D. were obliged to acknowledge the right of election in the states, and their power was restricted by a senate. Valdemar the Great was the only vigorous ruler of this dynasty; but succeeding conquerors, Canute VI. and Valdemar II. (1157 to 1223), while they spread their power upon the coasts of the Baltic, did not in the least advance the civilization of their own kingdom. Valdemar was made prisoner by Count Henry of Schwerin, and all the Danish conquests in Prussia and Courland were lost. When, in 1241, this prince divided his weakened kingdom amongst his sons, new internal divisions arose, while disputes with the powerful Hanseatic towns greatly weakened the power of the state. Upon the death of Valdemar and that of his grandson Olav IV., Margaret, Valdemar's daughter, assumed the reins of government in 1387. This princess, whose name is deservedly celebrated in the histories of the North, partly by marriage, and partly by conquest, united the crowns o. D. Norway, and Sweden, in 1388; an act which was solemnly ratified on the 12th July, 1397, by the union of Calmar, the articles of what leclared that the three states should remain for ever indissoluce, anited, but should each retain their own laws and privileges. This union, however, was unfortunate in its issue; and under Erick of Pomerania, the grandson of Margaret's sister, who reigned from 1412 to 1439, new troubles broke out in Sweden and the German provinces, which were not appeased by his abdication, nor under his successor Christopher of Bavaria, who reigned from 1439 to 1448. Middle history.] Christopher having died without an heir, Christian I., count of Oldenburg, the founder of the present royal family of Denmark, was elected by the Danes, and reigned from 1448 to 1481. This prince was obliged to conclude a treaty with the states, in which he acknowledged D. an elective kingdom. He united Norway, Sleswick, and Holstein, with the crown of D. His son John, who reigned from 1481 to 1513, ratified a constitution which still more fettered the royal prerogatives than that acceded to by his father; in Norway also his powers were considerably limited. He divided Holstein and Sleswick with his brother Frederic. Christian II., the son of John, an ambitious and cruel prince yet not without talent, sought to deprive the nobles of their privileges and independence; but the attempt cost him Sweden, which dissolved the union of Calmar in 1523; soon afterwards his two other crowns were wrested from him by the Danish and Norwegian nobility, who placed upon the throne his father's brother, Frederic I., duke of Sleswick and Holstein. This prince, who reigned till 1533, entered into alliance with Gustavus Vasa, king of Sweden, and with the town of Lubeck. He was obliged, however, to make many concessions to the powerful aristocracy who had elevated him to the throne; while the peasantry, oppressed and reduced almost to a state of bondage under the increasing preponderance of the nobility, broke out into frequent rebellions. After Frederic's death the throne remained vacant for a whole year, till the intrepid spirit shown by the citizens of Lubeck in prohibiting the navigation of the Sound to the Netherland merchants compelled the Danish aristocracy to accelerate the settlement of the government, and Christian mounted the throne in 1534. This prince divided Sleswick and Holstein with his brothers John and Adolph, the latter of whom became the founder of the house of Holstein-Gottorp. But this partition proved a fertile source of family disputes. In 1559 Frederic II. ascended the throne. This prince was involved in a war with Sweden on account of Livonia, which was put an end to by the peace of Stettin in 1570. Christian IV., on mounting the throne in 1588, renewed the war with Sweden, besides taking a very unfortunate part in the Thirty years' war. Under this prince, D. lost, in 1645, the territories of Jempteland and Herjedelen, and the two islands of Gotsland and Oesel; and Halland was likewise pledged for thirty years to Sweden. By the peace of Lubeck, in 1629, D. withdrew from the Thirty years' war after having been worsted by Tilly.-Christian was succeeded by his son Frederic in 1648, who having been induced by the Dutch to declare war against Charles Gustavus, in 1657, soon found that monarch a formidable enemy. Charles took Friedericstadt by assault, and, having led his army across the sea during winter, attacked the Danes in those islands which constituted the most valuable part of their territories. Frederic defended himself with much bravery, but might have found himself obliged to yield to the Swedes, had not Cromwell, the English protector, interposed in his favour. To purchase peace, however, Frederic was obliged to cede by the treaty of Roskild, the provinces of Halland, Blekingen, and Schonen: Bahus and Drontheim in Norway; and the island of Bornholm, to the Swedes. Frederic had, at first, consented to these terms, but he afterwards manifested a disposition to retract from them; whereupon Charles laid siege to the Danish capital. Frederic, whose courage and abilities seem to have been considerable, defended himself with intrepidity, till a Dutch fleet arriving in the Baltic, and discomfiting the fleet of the Swedes, compelled Charles to raise the siege of Copenhagen. The Danish king was now preparing to act the part of an aggressor, when the English, willing to enforce the performance of a treaty which had been formed by their mediation, appeared with a fleet on the side of Charles, and enabled him once more to lay siege to Copenhagen. More anxious, however, to reconcile the contending parties, than to protract hostilities, the English, in conjunction with the French, again offered their mediation for the re-establishment of peace, and after some negociation, a new treaty was concluded, by which Bahus, Blekingen, Halland, and Schonen, were ceded to the Swedes. Revolution.] Frederic, by the intrepidity of his character, had greatly endeared himself to the common people, and had confirmed their affection by granting them several privileges which had formerly been denied to the lower orders. Till 1660, the government of D. had been vested in the king, with the three estates of the kingdom, -the nobles, the clergy, and the commons. It would appear that the peasants, at least of the royal demesnes, had till the reign of Valdemar II. formed a fourth estate, as in Sweden and in the Tyrol. The power of the king was extremely limited; he was only the president of the privy council, a body composed of the great officers-of-state, and the commander-inchief of the forces. The monarchy was, by the constitution, elective; but the choice was confined to the reigning family, and generally fell on the elder son, or on the nearest male. The power of the nobles seems at this time to have been exorbitant, and, in the usual spirit of men vested with undue power, they wished still farther to extend their authority. When the treaty above-mentioned was concluded, an assembly of the states was summoned for the purpose of considering the condition of the kingdom, exhausted by the war which had been just terminated. This assembly met, for the first time since 1536, at Copenhagen, in the autumn of 1660. The commons had formerly been obliged to sustain the whole burden of the government, but they now foresaw that the burden would be by far too heavy. The nobles were, perhaps, convinced, that reason required the richest members of the state to contribute to its aid at such an emergency; and offered to contribute, but insisted that this concession should not be considered as a precedent, or subject them to future taxes To reject the offer of the nobles was not difficult, but still it was necessary to provide for the exigences of the state. To make this provision in an adequate manner, was scarcely possible for the commons. It was necessary that the nobles should contribute their share, a measure to which they would by no means consent, without the augmentation of their privileges, and this augmentation seemed to the two plebeian orders to endanger their remaining liberty. In such a situation, a desperate effort became necessary: and, accordingly, to remedy one evil they incurred another: to deprive the nobles of their power, the clergy and the commons declared the crown hereditary. The nobles rejected the bill; but these two orders laid their decree before the king, and being supported by the soldiery, the third order yielded to the power which it could not resist. On the 15th January, 1661, each of the three orders separately presented to the king a decree rendering the crown hereditary in the female as well as male line, and vesting him with the power of distributing all political authority in the state: and in 1665, the king, by virtue of the power conferred on him by the states, promulgated 'the royal law'-in imitation of the lex regia of the lawyers of imperial Rome-which has ever since been the only fundamental law of Denmark. By this law, the kings of D. were declared absolute. The effect of the new policy of D. did not answer expectation. Christian V. and Frederic IV. were alike unsuccessful in war with Sweden; and the attempt of Christian V., in 1686, to subdue the small state of Hamburg was equally unfortunate.-Frederic IV mounted the throne in 1699. He found the treasury exhausted. and the whole kingdom groaning under accumulated difficulties. But under his government the commerce of the kingdom revived, and the death of Charles in 1718 put a stop to the struggles with Sweden. In 1720 Frederic regained possession of Sleswick, with a sum of 600,000 dollars from Sweden, after which the kingdom enjoyed almost uninterrupted peace for one hundred years. Peace alone, however, cannot heal the wounds which misfortune has inflicted, and a bad form of government perpetuates. D. is a state of limited resources, and can only preserve its independence by a course of great prudence and energy.-Christian VI. succeeded Frederic IV. in 1730. Under his government the commerce of the kingdom increased a little. The Tranquebar company, erected in 1616, was renewed; and the West Indian company purchased the island of St. Croix, in the Antilles, from France.—Under his son Frederic V., who mounted the throne in 1746, and his able minister Bernstorff, the affairs and finances of the kingdom continued to revive. The lands of the duke of Holstein-Plen reverted to the Crown; but, on the other hand, on the accession of Peter III., duke of Holstein, to the throne of Russia, D. was threatened with war by that formidable power: Peter wishing to restore to his line the dukedom of Sleswick, to which the Danish monarch had laid claim. The Russian army were already assembled in Mecklenburg, and the Danes in Holstein, when Peter was driven from the throne. On Catherine's accession it was arranged, that her son the grand duke Paul, heir to the Holstein territory, should renounce his claim to that duchy in favour of D., and should receive in exchange from the latter power the counties of Oldenburg and Delmenhorst. This exchange was confirmed on the grand duke's attaining majority in 1773, so that from this period the whole of Sleswick and Holstein have belonged to D. Paul bestowed his new territories on the younger line of Gottorp in the person of the bishop of Lubeck. The great advantage gained by this annexation, was the removal of those grounds of differences which had so often been agitated between the different lines of Holstein and the kings of D.- Christian ascended the throne of D. in 1766. The early years of his government were disturbed by the affair with Struensee, a man of great talents, and a restless disposition, who had raised himself from the situation of king's physician to that of first minister. Struensee's administration soon became hateful to the nobility and ministry, and a plot having been formed for his overthrow, under the queen's mother, the favourite was disgraced, and soon afterwards brought to the scaffold, with his friend count Brand. The new ministry was displaced in 1784 by the Crown-prince on his assuming the regency during the derangement of the king. The head of the new ministry, Bernstorf, was an able man, and managed the external relations of the kingdom with considerable address. Among other wise acts of the regent, a decree was passed on 16th March, 1792, which declared that from and after the commencement of 1803, the slave-trade should be held illegal throughout his Danish majesty's dominions. D. had thus the honour of being the first of the European powers which proscribed that infamous traffic. During the French revolution, D. preserved a strict neutrality, but two Danish frigates having been brought into British ports under English search warrants, D. and Sweden joined the northern armed neutrality on the 16th Dec., 1800, whereupon the English ministry laid an embargo on the ships of these nations, and seized the colonies of both kingdoms. At the same time admirals Parker and Nelson, with a fleet of 47 ships-of-war, appeared in the Baltic. The Swedish fleet of 7 ships-of-the-line and 3 frigates lay immoveable at Carlscrona, while the British fleet passed through the Sound. The Russian fleet could not put to sea, on account of the season; in the meanwhile the death of Paul changed the politics of Russia, so that D. was left singlehanded to cope with the power of Great Britain. The Danish fleet lay in the inner harbour of Copenhagen; but a part of it, under Admiral Fisher, defended themselves so honourably, that Nelson, after a bloody fight, on the 2d April, 1801, granted the Danes an honourable armistice. On the 23d October, 1801, D. and Russia entered into a treaty with England, whereupon the latter power restored the Danish colonies in the East and West Indies; while the Danes, on the other hand, withdrew their troops from Hamburg and Lubeck. In 1805, when the fall of Prussia had paved the way for an attack on Swedish Pomerania, and every thing was to be apprehended from the general policy of the French ruler, the Crown-prince drew together his troops in Holstein and Sleswick; but the storm swept past the boundaries of D. without touching them, the battle of Austerlitz having decided the war before the struggle in the North could begin. In the following year, upon the formation of the Rhenish confederation, and the dissolution of the German empire, the duchy of Holstein, which had formerly been restored to Germany, was declared to form an integral part of D. In October, 1806, the French had advanced their armies to Lubeck; but had not yet infringed upon the neutrality of the Danish territory, when the British ministry projected the seizure of the Danish fleet, under the pretext - not unfounded - that it had been arranged at Tilsit to force D. into an alliance against Great Britain, and to shut the Sound. A British fleet of 18 ships-of-the-line and 7 frigates, commanded by Admiral Gambier, having on board 28,000 troops, under the command of Lord Cathcart, entered the Sound, seeming at first destined to co-operate with the Swedes in the defence of Stralsund. This illusion was, however, dissipated on the approach of the fleet, and the arrival of Mr. Jackson, the English plenipotentiary, at Copenhagen, who demanded the delivery of the whole Danish fleet, the arsenal, and the fortress of Cronenberg in deposit to the British. These demands were rejected with indignation by the Crown-prince; whereupon, on the 16th of August, the British armament disembarked at Webeck and Kitige in Sieland, and proceeded to invest Copenhagen. The bombardment began on the morning of the 2d September, and lasted till the evening of the 5th, when 400 houses being laid in ashes, and 2,000 inhabitants killed, the Danish general, Peymann, capitulated, consenting to give up the Danish fleet to the custody of England in the course of six weeks, till a general peace should be concluded. While this treaty was carrying into execution, the English remained in possession of Copenhagen and the two Holms; and on the 21st October the British sailed from Sieland, taking along with them the Danish fleet, consisting of 18 ships-of-theline, 15 frigates, 6 brigs, and 25 gun-boats, after having destroyed the docks. During these transactions Sweden remained a quiet spectator; but France, Russia, and Austria, protested against the line of conduct adopted by the British ministry, and on the 20th November, 1807, D. entered into an alliance with France, and declared war against England, and afterwards against Sweden. The Danish colonies, including Iceland, were thereupon seized by the English; but the Swedish attack upon Norway in 1808 was repulsed.—The death of Christian VII., which happened on the 13th March, 1808, effected no change on the political system of D., as his son Frederick VI. had in reality governed for the last 24 years. The change in the government of Sweden in 1809, was followed by a peace with D, on the status quo. The death of Christian Augustus, prince of Sleswick-Holstein, who had been named Crown-prince of Sweden, occasioned another change: for although Bernadotte, who was then named his successor, declared war in 1810 against Great Britain, he changed his system on finding himself placed between the two great allied powers of Britain and Russia, from whom he had most to fear or hope. In 1812 he entered into treaties with England, and sought, by an alliance with the two powers, to obtain Norway from his weaker neighbour the king of D., as a recompense for Finland. Frederic VI. had already entered into negociation with England. In 1813 he found himself compelled to defend Hamburg against the French; but Count Bernstorff returning at the same moment from London, after an unsuccessful negociation, and Napoleon gaining the victories of Lutzen and Bautzen, D., on the 18th July, renewed her alliance with France, and engaged to declare war against Sweden, Russia, and Prussia, the two latter powers continuing to insist on the surrender of Norway to Sweden. The Danish army, in concert with the French, now occupied Lubeck, After the battle of Leipsig, the Crown-prince of Sweden penetrated into Holstein, and compelled the Danes to enter into the peace of Kiel with Sweden and Great Britain, in which it was stipulated that Norway should be given to Sweden, and the island of Heligoland to England. In return, D. received from Sweden an acknowledgment of the rights of the Sound; the renunciation of the claims which Sweden had upon D., amounting to 12,000,000 of francs; the evacuation of Sleswick and Holstein, and the surrender of Pomerania; and from England the cession of Tranquebar. In February 1814, D. concluded peace with Russia; and in June 1815, obtained Lauenburg and a sum of money from Prussia in exchange for Pomerania and Rugen. In the same year the king joined the German confederation, in which he possesses 3 votes for Holstein and Lauenberg.-Frederic VI. died in Dec. 1839, and was succeeded by Christian VIII., born in Sept. 1786, who ascended the throne with a high character, and proved himself a popular monarch. By successive acts, in 1831, and 1834, he established estates, or representative assemblies, in the several provs. of the Danish kingdom: and in these the liberal party went on each year demanding and obtaining fresh concessions. Indeed, any liberal progress in D. was soon cut short by the divisions and jealousies between the German and Danish parties in the duchies, but especially in Sleswick. On one occasion the great dispute was what language should prevail. Frederic VI. had favoured the Danish, and now the Germans sought to forbid the Danish altogether. The late king, on this question being referred to him, decided that each member should speak in the tongue which he knew best,-a decision that was considered very pusillanimous, and gave general dissatisfaction. A more serious subject of discussion arose in 1842, and gave rise to much heat in 1844, viz., the right of succession. As the throne of D. passed to female heirs, and as Holstein was a male fief, the question was, how should the succession be regulated, both with respect to Sleswick and to Holstein? At length the king came forward in 1846, and by letters patent declared that Sleswick must remain indubitably attached to the Danish monarchy; but that, as to Holstein, the right of succession was doubtful. This concession, instead of being met with respect by the Holsteiners, excited their utmost indignation. They did not wish to have their fate separated from that of Sleswick, and their remonstrances were loud; so much so, that Christian was disgusted with forming projects for preserving intact the present empire of D. To this policy his successor, Frederic VII., born 1808, is considered to be peculiarly attached. The present sovereign married first the Princess Wilhelmina Mary, daughter of Frederic VI., from whom he was divorced in 1837 without issue. He afterwards married the Princess Caroline of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, and is now suing for a divorce, not having any issue. If the prince royal dies without an heir, the brother of the king, Prince Frederic Ferdinand, will ascend the throne. He married a daughter of Frederic VI., and has no issue. In the event of the death of the prince royal and of Prince Frederic, the male line of D. will be extinct, and the crown will pass, according to the royal law of 1665, to the eldest sister of the king, Princess Juliana, born 18th February, 1788, and widow of Prince William of Hesse-Philippsthal; and on her decease-for she has no children-to her sister, Princess Charlotte, wife of the Landgrave William of Hesse-Cassel. The letter patent of the late king of D., in 1846, argues that the right of succession to the duchies devolves to the king's sister, with the exception of a slight portion of Holstein The inhabitants of Sleswick and Holstein, on the contrary, uphold that when Christian I., of the house of Oldenhurg, and king of D., was elected duke and count of Sleswick and of Holstein and Storman in 1460, he was not elected as king of D., and that, according to agreement, the states have the right of choosing among his children, if not among his legitimate heirs, and moreover these countries are always to remain united. At a later period the right of primogeniture was substituted for that of election in the male line of Christian I., and the first heir of this male line is Duke Christian Augustus of Sleswick-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg, whose claims to the succession to the duchy of Holstein, and to Sleswick, are supported by the German party in the duchies. Christian had three sons; it is from the eldest, Frederic II., king of D., that the present royal family of D. descends. The second brother, John, is the head of the house of Holstein-Sonderburg, and his heir is Duke of Augustenburg, lieutenant-general of the Danish army. The junior branch of the house of Holstein-Sonderburg, which follows that of Augustenburg, is the ducal branch of Sleswick-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg, and counts a number of princes. After the two branches of Holstein-Sonderburg comes the line of Holstein-Gottorp. The head of this house is Prince Adolphus of D., brother of King Christian III. Descended from the elder branch of this line, Charles Peter Ulrich, son of Duke Charles Frederic and Anne, daughter of Peter I. of Russia, ascended the Russian throne as Peter III. His son, Paul I., father of the present emperor, made over his portion of Holstein-Gottorp to Christian of D., in exchange for the counties of Oldenburg and Delmenshorst (Nov. 16, 1773), and made over those lands to the junior episcopal branch of Lubeck, of the house of Holstein and Gottorp, the present reigning family of Oldenburg, the youngest branch of the imperial house of Russia, on whose behalf it is understood the Russian court is now seeking to set aside the claims of the two ducal houses of Augustenburg and Glücksburg to the eventual succession to the duchies. DENMARK, a river of South-western Australia, which descends from Mount Lindesay (Peepe-tup), on the E confines of Stirling co.; runs SE into Plantagenet co.; and falls into Wilson's inlet.

DENMARK, a township of Oxford co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 82 m. SW of Augusta, bordered on the SW by Saco river. It is extremely fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,143.—Also a township of Lewis co., in the state of New York, 146 m. NW of Albany, watered by Black river on its NE border, and by Deer creek. It has a hilly surface. The soil consists of a rich loam, with a substratum of limestone. Pop. 2,388.—Also a township of Ashtabula co., in the state of Ohio, 208 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 176.
DENMEAD, a tything in the p. of Hambleton, Hants. Pop. 567. DEN
DENN, a parish of co. Cavan, 54 m. SE of Cavan, Area 11,600 acres. Pop. 6,696.
DENN, a town of Arabia, in Yemen, 90 m. SSW of Sana. It is defended by a fortress.
DENNANT, a tithe township in the p. of Llansaintffraid, Denbighshire. Pop. 152.
DENNAP, a cluster of tokuls or villages, on the L bank of the Nile, in the Shilluk territory, in about 10° N lat. It was represented to Werne as forming the cap. of the Shilluk country.
DENNEWITZ, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, regency of Potsdam, circle and 2 m. SW of Juterbook - Luckenwalde. — A victory was gained here over the French by the allies in 1813.
DENNINGTON, a parish of Suffolk, 2 m. N of Framlingham, at the source of the Alde. Area 2,840 acres. Pop. 979.

DENNIS, a maritime township of Barnstable co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 81 m. SE of Boston. extending across Cape Cod. It has an active coasting trade, and extensive fisheries. Pop. in 1840, 2,942.—Also a township of Cape May co., in the state of New Jersey, 85 m. S of Trenton, bordered on the SW by Dennis creek, which flows into Delaware bay. Pop. 1,350.
DENNIS (SAINT), a parish of Cornwall, 41 m. SSE of St. Columb Major. Area 3,370 acres. Pop. 828.
DENNISON, a township of Luzerne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 976.

DENNY, a parish in the shire of Stirling, computed to contain 6,016 acres. It is bounded on the N by the Carron. Pop. in 1801, 2,033; in 1841, 4,916. There are paper-mills, wool spinning-mills, and bleachfields in this p.—The v. of D., in the NE part of the p., is 73 m. NE of Stirling. Pop. in 1841. 1,881.—Besides the v. of D., the p. contains those of Haggs, Denny-Loanhead, and Bankier.
DENNYSVILLE, a township of Washington eo., in the state of Maine, U. S., 174 m. ENE of Augusta, bordered on the SW by Dennis river, an affluent of Cobscook bay. Pop. in 1840, 378.
DENOVAN, a village in the p. of Dunipace, Stirlingshire. Pop. 104.
DENOWAN, a headland on the SW coast of the island of Mindanao, Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 6 25', E long. 124° 5'. To the E of this cape is an islet of the same name.
DENSHANGER, a hamlet in the p. of Passenham, Northamptonshire. Pop. 606.
DENSHIKOVA. See DENJIKOVO.
DENSTON, a township in the p. of Alton, Staffordshire, 5 m. N of Uttoxeter, in a beautiful vale on the W bank of the Churnet, and intersected by the Uttoxeter canal. Pop. 231.
DENT, a chapelry in the p. of Sedbergh, W. R. of Yorkshire, 73 m. NNE of Kirkby-Lonsdale. Area 19,210 acres. Pop. 1,887.
DENTA, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Temes, 30 m. S of Temeswar, on the L bank of the Berzava, at the junction of the canal of that name. Pop. 3,664. Rice is cultivated in the district.
DENTERGHEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of West Flanders, 13 m. NE of Courtray. Pop. 2,768. It has extensive linen manufactories.

DENTILLA, a territory of Senegambia, extending from the Fatima on the E, to the Gambia on the W, and intersected by the parallel of 13° N. It is generally mountainous, and is watered by several torrents. It is inhabited by Mandingoes. Gold and iron occur in considerable quantities in this region.

DENTON, a parish of Huntingdonshire, 1 m. SSW of Stilton, to the S of Holme-Brook. Area 890 acres. Pop. 97.-Also a p. of Kent, 81 m. SSE of Canterbury, near the post-road from Canterbury to Dover. Area 1,160 acres. Pop. 208.—Also a small | p. of the same co. Area 660 acres. Pop. 148.- Also a p. of Lincolnshire, 4 m. SW of Grantham, near the Grantham and Nottingham canal. Area 2,730 acres. Pop. 626.—Also a p. of Norfolk, 4½ m. SE of Harleston, on the N bank of the Waveney. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 625.—Also a p. of Northamptonshire, 6 m. ESE of Northampton. Area 1,970 acres. Pop. 557.—Also a p. of Sussex, 12 m. NE of Newhaven, bounded on the W by the Ouse. Area 890 acres. Pop. 120.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Gainford, co. of Durham, 51 m. NW of Darlington, on a branch of the Skerne. Area 800 acres. Pop. 119 .- Also a chapelry in the p. of Manchester, Lancashire, 4 m. NNE of Stockport, W of the Tame, and near the Manchester and Sheffield railway. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 3,440.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Cuddesden, Oxfordshire, 61 m. NW of Tetsworth. Area 730 acres. Pop. 163.-Also a chapelry in the p. and 4 m. NW of Otley, W. R. of Yorkshire, N of the Warfe. Area 3,170 acres. Pop. 185.
DENTON, a village of Caroline co., in the state of Maryland, U. S., 65 m. E of Annapolis, on the E bank of Choptank river.
DENTON (East and West), townships in the p. of Newburn, Northumberland. The former is 31 m. WNW of Newcastle-upon-Tyne. Pop. 543; pop. of latter 420.
DENTON (NETHER), a parish of Cumberland, 5 m. ENE of Brampton, on the Irthing, and intersected by the Carlisle and Newcastle railway. Area 860 acres. Pop. 127.
DENVER, a parish of Norfolk, 1 m. S of Market Downham, on the post-road from Cambridge to Kingslynn. Area 2,610 acres. Pop. 910.
DENWICK, a township in the p. of Alnwick, Northumberland. Pop. 210.
DENZLINGEN, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, and 4 m. S of Emmendingen. Pop. 1,500, of whom 27 are Catholics.

DEOBUN, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 75 m. NNE of Delhi, district and 24 m. SSE of Seharanpur. It is of considerable extent, and contains 2 mosques, a serai, and the remains of an old fort. The houses in the principal streets are built of brick, and are chiefly inhabited by Brahmins.

DEO-DHOORA, or DEODURA, a temple of Hindostan, in the prov. of Delhi, 20 m. SE of Almora, situated at an alt. of 6,780 ft. above sea-level, in the vertical opening of a rock, and accessible only by a passage which penetrates horizontally the body of the rock. Adjacent is a village of the same name, occupying a romantic position on the brink of a finely wooded precipice. A small station has been erected here by government for the accommodation of travellers to the Lohn Ghaut, the road thence from Almora running past the village.
DEODHUR, or DEODUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, 20 m. NNE of Rhadempur.
DEOGHIR. See DOWLUTABAD.
DEOGHUR, or DEOGARH, a district of India, in the Nagpur territory, in Gundwana, now included in the district of Chanda.

DEOGHUR, a district of Birbhum, in the prov. of Bengal, on the great table-land which extends from near Burdwan to Dunwa Ghaut in Behar. There are indications of valuable ores in this district. Granite and gneiss, traversed by greenstone veins, the usual matrices of copper and lead veins all over the world, abound in the vicinity. Near the vein is a mountain-torrent which dries up in the fair season. The surface of the district is undulating, and the drainage seems easy. The metals do not appear ever to have been worked or made use of. The vein at the surface runs E and W, and has been traced for P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P DEP 100 ft. continuously; the metal is partly pure, partly in the form of green carbonate of copper. Veins of lead ore, partly pure, partly in the state of galena or sulphuret, traverse the principal vein at right angles. Both have been analyzed and found of remarkable richness. The whole question of the value of the discovery consists in the expense of raising the ores and converting them into metal. The nearest coal is 40 m. off, and the nearest point on the Ganges where the ore could be shipped to Calcutta is 60 m. distant, with a tolerable road all the way.—The town of D., or Baidyanath, is in N lat. 24° 32', 35 m. NNE of Serampur, and 20 m. E by S of Chacki. See Bai- DYANATH.

DEOGHUR, or DEOGURH, a village and hill-fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, division of Ujjain, 7 m. SE by S of Sikur. It is built on a high, steep, and rocky ridge, running from NNE to SSW, which is the general direction of the chain which traverses Shekawati, of which this ridge is an integral part, though not immediately connected with the great mass. The road up is on the SE side of the hill, and consists of a series of zig-zags, about 10 ft. wide, and constructed of stone. The summit is between 700 and 800 ft. above the plain. The rock is quartz-clay, slate, and mica-slate. The fort consists of a double enclosure, of which the centre, occupying the highest part of the summit, is pretty regularly planned; but the exterior works adapt themselves to the rocky winding ground on which they stand. The interior fort is a parallelogram, of which the half towards the NNE is much lower than the other towards the SSW. The fort is a fine object as seen from Rewasa, Sikur, &c.; and commands an extensive prospect over the sandy downs of Shekawati. Kho, or Rughunathgurh, is seen to the NE; Rewasa fort to the E, on a parallel ridge about 500 ft. high, and 2 m. distant; Luchmungurh, Khuror, &c., are also visible; but the most conspicuous object is the lofty hill and temple of Bhiruji or Hurshnath, bearing S by W about 5 m.
DEOGURH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 32 m. SE of Gwalior, near the l. bank of the Sinde.
DEOHRA, a town of Northern Hindostan, in the states of Gurhwal, district of Sirmur, near the l. bank of the Pabur.

DEOLEEA, or Dewla, a fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 8 m. E of Purtabgurh, at an alt. of 1,770 ft. above sea-level. It contains some good houses, and in 1820 was the residence of the Purtabgurh raja. DE'OLS, Bourg-de-De'ols, or Bourg-Dieu, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre, cant. and 1 m. NNE of Chateauroux, on the r. bank of the Indre. Pop. in 1841, 2,344. It is of great antiquity, and was formerly the cap. of the principality of the same name, called also Bas-Berry. It has a rich abbey, which was secularized in 1613.
DEONELLY, or DEONHULLY, a fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 23 m. NNE of Bangalore, to the W of the Penaukeny.
DEOPHAM, a parish of Norfolk, 33 m. SW of Wymondham. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 525.
DEOS-TE-LIVRE, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, one of the numerous ramifications of the Cordelheira-de-Mantequeira.
DEO-TAL, a small lake of Northern Hindostan, in the range of the Himalaya mountains, 26 m. ENE of Gangoutri, in N lat. 31° 4', and E long. 79° 24'.

DEPALPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Punjab, in the prov. and 75 m. SSW of Lahore, situated in the doab of the Bari, on the r. side of the supposed former bed of the Beas, in N lat. 22° 50'. Pop. in 1820, 5,175. It was formerly the cap. of a district. The locality is noted for its breed of horses.
DEPANVILLE, a village of Clayton township, Jefferson co., in the state of New York, U.S., 178 m. NW of Albany, on the E side of Chaumont river. Pop. in 1840, 200.
DEPDEN, a parish of Suffolk, 7 m. SW of Bury- St.-Edmonds. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 345.
DEPEHDELEN. See TEPELENI.

DEPEYSTER, a township of Lawrence co., in the state of New York, U. S., 198 m. NNW of Albany, bordered on the W by Black lake, and intersected by Oswegatchie river. It has an undulating surface, and is very fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,074.
DEPOSIT, a village of Tompkins township, Delaware co., in the state of New York, U.S., 118 m. WSW of Albany, on the N side of Delaware river. Pop. in 1840, 600.
DEPPEN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of East Prussia, regency of Königsberg, and circle of Heilsberg, on the r. bank of the Passarge, 11 m. SW of Guttstadt. Pop. 72.

DEPTFORD, a large town on the S bank of the Thames, at the mouth of the small river Ravensbourne, in the hund. of Blackheath, Kent, 5 m. E of London, and 11 m. W by N of Dartford, intersected by the Croydon canal which here joins the Surrey canal, by the Croydon railway, and by the Greenwich railway which runs through the High-street on a viaduct. The town is very irregularly built. It is nominally divided into Upper and Lower D. The latter, originally called West Greenwich and D. Strond, was at one time merely a small fishing-village. The entire town was of little importance till Henry established a naval dock-yard here, and incorporated the society of Trinity-house, to examine the mathematical students of Christ's hospital, and masters in the navy; to settle the rates of pilotage, and to erect lighthouses, &c. The ancient Trinityhall at D. was pulled down about the year 1787, when the Trinity-house was erected at London, where the society now holds its meetings. The royal dock-yard now encloses an area of about 31 acres. It has a double wet dock of 2 acres, and a single dock of 11 acre, in area. There are large workshops of all descriptions requisite for fitting-out ships-of-war, a basin, 2 mast-ponds, and numerous iron-forges. The capacious storehouses, including the victualling - office, originally built in 1745, but burnt in 1749, have been re-erected; and now consist of numerous ranges of buildings, all appropriated to the various establishments necessary to the important office of victualling the navy. The victualling-yard supplies the whole of the government - establishments abroad with provisions, clothing, and medicines. All the transports and convict ships are also fitted-out at this yard, under the inspection of a resident agent. The residences of the presiding officers are also included in the edifices connected with this establishment. All the affairs of the dock-yard and its appendages are under the control of the admiralty, and the immediate superintendence of a captain in the navy, who is called Superintendent, and holds office for 5 years. In time of war, from 1,000 to 1,500 workmen have been employed here. There are several private dock-yards for boat and ship-building, including Evelyn's dock, where men-of-war of 74 guns are sometimes built; also a commodious commercial dock, intended for foreign merchantmen in the Baltic trade. About a 1 m. from the town was an extensive erection wherein bayonets, gun-barrels, halberts, &c., were manufactured by machinery set in motion by a powerful steam-engine: this building is now converted into a mill for throwing silk. A ropework upon the vertical principle in the dock-yard, which will be sufficient for the supply of the whole navy with cables and ropes of any length, has been DER erected. The pop. of D. in 1801 was 17,548; in 1831, 21,350; in 1841, 23,165.—By the reform act, D. is conjoined with Greenwich, Woolwich, Chorlton, and Plumstead, in returning 2 members to parliament.

DEPTFORD, a township of Gloucester co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., drained by Big-Timber creek and branches of Egg-Harbour river. It has a level surface, and is to a great extent covered with timber. Pop. in 1840, 2,570. Woodbury, the cap. of the co., is situated in this township.

DEPUCH ISLAND, an island of the Southern ocean, the centre of a string of islands near the N coast of De Witt Land, in NW Australia, to the E of Dampier archipelago, in S lat. 20° 37' 47", and W long. of Swan river 2° 0′ 20". It is "a level lump of land," as seen from seaward, about 6 m. in length, and almost entirely destitute of vegetation, excepting coarse grass and a few stunted eucalypti. It consists of a pile of reddish coloured blocks; and attains an elevation of 514 ft. The mainland, at the distance of from 2 to 3 m., is here flat for a great distance inland, with a few isolated rocky hills.
DER. See DEHR and DEIR.

DERA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 95 m. SE of Lucknow, on the l. bank of the Gumti. DERABUND, DARRABAND, or DRABUND, a small town of Afghanistan, in the Derajat district, on an affluent of the Indus, 30 m. WNW of Dera-Ismailkhan, in N lat. 31° 45', and E long. 70° 18'; finely situated on the elevated bank of a hill stream. It now consists of only a few ill-built houses, with a permanent pop. not exceeding 1,000; but is of importance as the place of rendezvous of the caravans which winter in the Derajat, to obtain supplies of the merchandise of India for the trade of Central Asia. This town has suffered much from the attacks of the Vaziri hordes, and other predatory tribes, which frequent the locality.

DERA-DIN-PUNAH, a district and town of the Punjab, on an inosculation of the Indus, on the l. bank of that river, 25 m. S of Leia, on the road to Multan. The district is fertile.—Also a town of Afghanistan, in the Derajat, on the r. bank of the Indus. It was to a great extent destroyed by an earthquake and inundation which took place in 1819.

DERA-FATI-KHAN, a town of Afghanistan, in the Derajat, 25 m. NW of Leia, near the confluence of a small western affluent of the Indus. Pop. 5,000, chiefly Mahommedans. It has good and well-stocked bazaars, and a thriving trade. It takes its name from Fati-khan, an adventurer who, towards the middle of the 16th cent., located himself here.

DERA-GHAZI-KHAN, a district and town of Afghanistan, in the Derajat, on an inosculation of the Indus, about 4 m. from the W bank of that river, 65 m. WNW of Multan, and 300 m. SE of Candahar, in N lat. 29° 55', and E long. 70° 30'. Pop. - consisting of nearly equal numbers of Hindus and Mahommedans - 25,000. This town, the most southerly and most important of the three towns from which the Derajat derives its name, is still of great extent, but from the number of ruined mosques and handsome edifices which are to be found in it, is evidently declining in prosperity. It has a commodious and well-stocked though ill-built bazaar, and possesses extensive manufactories of articles in silk and cotton, and of common cutlery. The advantages of its situation, at the intersection of the great Northern and Eastern routes, render its transit trade-which is here, in common with the other towns in the Derajat, almost exclusively carried on by the Lohani Afghans—extremely active. The surrounding country is unhealthy, but well-irrigated and extremely fertile, producing indigo in great abundance, cotton, grain, fruit, sugar, &c. The town owes its foundation to Ghazi-khan, one of the three adventurers who, towards the middle of the 16th cent., formed encampments in the Derajat.

DERA-ISMAIL-KHAN, a town of Afghanistan, cap. of the prov. of Daman, in the Derajat, 130 m. NNW of Multan, and 135 m. NNE of Dera-Ghazikhan, pleasantly situated in a plantation of date-trees, on an inosculation of the Indus, near the ferry of Kaheri—one of the most frequented over that river, and on one of the principal routes from the N into Sinde, in N lat. 31° 53', and E long. 70° 52'. Pop., consisting of Baluchis, Afghans, Hindus, and Juts -but chiefly of the first-8,000. It is about 1 m. in circumf., and is well laid out; but the houses, as well as the wall by which the town is enclosed, are unsubstantially built of unburnt brick, and the general aspect of the place-except at the period of the annual arrival of the Lohani caravans-is one of desolation. The transit as well as the direct trade of the Lohanis is here carried on with great activity. As many as 2,000,000 yards of white calico are annually sold here, and 18,000,000 yards of the same article are transported hence for the supply of the countries to the N and E. A considerable trade in grain and salt is also maintained by means of the Indus with the districts to the south. The original town of this name-which owed its existence to the adventurer whose name it bears-was situated about a hundred yards from the W bank of the Indus, and was swept away by an inundation of that river.
DERAH, an island of the Persian gulf, at the entrance to the estuary of the principal embouchure of the Karun, in N lat. 30° 10′, and E long. 19° 10′.

DERAJAT, an extensive plain of Afghanistan, stretching between the W bank of the Indus to the range of the Suliman mountains, and from the Kala, or Salt range, on the N, to the N confines of Sinde. Its soil is well-cultivated and extremely fertile; but the chief importance of this district is derived from the great roads by which it is intersected, and from its commanding several of the most frequented ferries over the Indus. The name is derived from its three principal towns—originally the deras or camps of the chiefs-Ismail-khan, Fati-khan, and Ghazikhan, whose names they respectively bear.

DERAJINA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Podolia, district and 10 m. SW of Letitchey, on the l. bank of the Dorajna. Pop. 1,000.-Also a town in the gov. of Volhynia, district and 18 m. NW of Rovno, on a small river 5 m. S of its confluence with the Gorin. Pop. 500.

DE RAMZAY, a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. of Berthier. It is to a considerable extent incapable of cultivation. The village is near the river Assomption, and consists of about 30 houses .- Also a seignory in the co. of St. Hyacinth, and bounded on the SW by the seignory of that name. It is nearly altogether uncultivated, but appears, from the quality of its timber, to possess a good soil. In the S it is watered by the Chibouet. Pop. 386.

DERAYEH (EL), a town of Arabia, cap. of the Nedjed, and of the prov. of El Aredh, 260 m. WSW of El Katif, on the Persian gulf, and 440 m. ENE of Mecca, in N lat. 25° 15', and E long. 46° 9'. Pop. 13,000. It lies in a fertile valley, named Wady- Hanyseh, at the foot of Mount Khur-a branch of the Toneyk range-in a situation of considerable strength, the only access being from the W by the narrow pass of El Chekke. It consists of 5 parts, each surrounded by a bastioned wall. The N section contains the palace of the imam; the NE the residences of the ecclesiastical and legal functionaries. It possesses 28 mosques without minarets, 30 medressas or schools, and several bazaars. The principal houses are constructed of stone, but brick forms DER the common material.—This town was formerly the stronghold of the Wahabys. In 1819 it was taken and almost entirely destroyed by Ibrahim Pasha. The valley is well-watered and fertile, producing grain and fruit in abundance.

DE'RBEND, a small principality or khanship, formerly of Persia, now comprised in the Russian prov. of Georgia; bounded on the N by the river Derbak, or Kerebagh; on the E by the Caspian sea; on the S by the rivers Kur and Salian; and on the W by the district of Tabasseran. It extends about 20 m. in length, by 15 m. in breadth; is well watered, and of a mountainous surface. The soil is exceedingly fertile, wheat yielding 20 fold, and rice double that produce; fine grapes are also grown, but the wine made of them is not good. Some silk and woollen manufactures are carried on by the inhabitants. Pop. in 1843, 37,000. This khanship owes its consequence entirely to the strong and important position of the town of Derbend, its capital.

DERBEND, a town, the cap. of the above khanship, situated on the W coast of the Caspian sea, in N lat. 42° 5′ 45", E long. 47° 39′ 15", 135 m. NW of Baku. It is in the form of a parallelogram, about 3 m. long, by 1 m. wide, extending along an acclivity from the margin of the shore, W to the foot of a steep spur of the Caucasus. To the W of this there is no passage, unless by penetrating far among the mountains, which are guarded by tribes who have yet refused subjection to the authority of any civilized nation. It is however reported that within these few years Russia has succeeded in occupying these important defiles. D. is thus considered the gate of Georgia in this quarter; and its name signifies an impassable place,' or, more strictly, 'a locked door.' It is surrounded by strong walls, which on the N and S sides rise out of the sea, and are strengthened by round and square towers. These walls are continued along the steep part of the hill, the highest part of which is crowned by a fort or citadel, forming the upper part of the town, and 3 m. from the shore. Many of the stones used in the fortifications are cubes of 6 ft.; but the ramparts are so narrow that cannon are mounted only on the towers, and the entrance to the town is through a single antique iron gate. The town is divided into three quarters, of which the citadel, about half-a-mile square, constitutes the upper part, and is incomparably the strongest. The walls of the middle part of the town are above 30 ft. high; nearly 20 ft. thick at the foundation, and 12 or 15 ft. in the upper part: having a breastwork of about 3 ft. thick, and port-holes at convenient distances, for muskets, or bows and arrows. It is also defended by about 60 towers, chiefly square. In 1722, there were 230 pieces of cannon mounted on the walls; but in 1796 only 15. The town is completely commanded by the citadel. The streets are irregular, and the houses, almost universally of one story, have a terraced roof, being built quite in the eastern fashion. They are plentifully supplied with water, which was formerly brought to them by a beautiful aqueduct, now in ruins: it is now conveyed by pipes, or supplied from wells. The harbour of D., which scarcely merits the name, is the worst on the Caspian sea. It is so shallow that only boats can land. Formerly it had a quay, which is now sunk below the water .- D. is inhabited by Mahommedans, Georgians, Armenians, and Jews, who probably do not in all exceed 12,000. Little trade is carried on, owing to the poverty of the surrounding countries; but saffron is extensively cultivated in the neighbourhood, and the gardens are very fine. To the W of D. are the relics of a great wall which, it is said, extends along the whole chain of Tabasseran. It commences on the S angle of Fort Marym- Kalah, and runs from E to W, over the heights and along the ravines. It is built of bricks 21 ft. in length, 13 ft. wide, and 1 ft. thick. They are merely placed one upon another, without cement; but the interior is filled up with flints and stones which are united with a mixture of clay and lime. - D. is a place of great antiquity. The inhabitants pride themselves on a tradition that it was built by Alexander the Great; and, in corroboration of this fact, cite an inscription which no longer exists. The celebrated caliph Haroun-al-Reschid lived here at different times, and brought the town into great repute by promoting commerce and the arts. It has frequently changed masters. The Russians first took possession of it in 1722, and retained it until 1735, when it was restored to the Persians. Afterwards, it was subdued and possessed by Feth Ali. In 1796, the empress of Russia having declared war against the Persians, Count Valerian Subow entered Daghistan, at the head of an army; and having reconnoitred D., he ordered an assault, but the town surrendered without a struggle. In 1813 it shared the fate of Georgia in being annexed to the empire of the Czar.

DERBENT, a village of Turkey in Bulgaria, at the entrance of a mountain-gorge through which the road to Shumla passes.-Also a town of Turkey in Europe, in Bosnia, in the sanjak and 60 m. NNE of Trawnik, and 10 m. SW of Brod, on the r. bank of the Okrina.

DERBENT-I-BAZIAN, a pass in the Bazian range of hills in Kurdistan, leading from the plains of Assyria. It is formed by a mere ridge or wall which advances as it were to close the valley, and slopes down gradually, leaving but a small opening.
DERBESAK, a town of Syria, in the pash. and 25 m. NW of Aleppo, and 20 m. W of Iskenderun, on the r. bank of the Aswad.

DERBY, a parliamentary borough and markettown, the cap. of Derbyshire, in the hund. of Morleston and Litchurch, 29 m. NW of Leicester; 153 m. WSW of Nottingham; and 126 m. NW of London; chiefly seated on the W bank of the river Derwent, formerly navigable by a very tortuous course to the Trent, but has ceased to be used for purposes of traffic since navigable canals were cut to the Trent. The Markeaton brook flows through the town and joins the Derwent on its E suburb. The high-road from London to Manchester passes through the town, which is also in communication with an extensive tract of canals, and constitutes the central and common station of various highly important railways, branches of the Midland counties railway. D. is finely situated in a level fertile plain, surrounded by beautiful scenery. It is "a town at once of the present and the past; pervaded by the quaint air of antiquity, and yet instinct with the living energy of an actual and a bustling prosperity." The streets of the older part are crooked and narrow, but the new streets are well-built. There are six good stone bridges across Markeaton brook, and two modern and elegant bridges of three arches over the Derwent, which, with the silk-mills, the weirs, and the broad expanse of the river, form a very pleasing prospect on entering the town from the Nottingham road. A large open market - place occupies the centre of the town, and in this quarter is a spacious assembly-room, and a covered market. The townhall, a handsome edifice erected in 1828, was dedestroyed by fire in 1841, but has since been rebuilt. The county-hall is partly a new and commodious modern building. The streets are remarkably clean and well-paved. Since 1831, very considerable additions have been made to the buildings of this busy and flourishing town. "In point of building arrangements, the working pop. of D. are very decidedly better off than their neighbours at Nottingham. D., in fact, has always had more elbow-room. Its suburbs spread freely forth, and the town exhibits none of that structural piling and huddling characteristic of the cap. of the lace trade. Back-to-back dwellings exist, but they by no means constitute the rule; and towards the suburbs, and in most of the more recently constructed blocks of buildings, the presence of back doors gives facilities for full and free houseventilation. The smallest class of houses contains two apartments, one above another, and the average rent is 2s., although they sometimes fetch 2s. 3d., or, according to situation, 2s. 6d. Tenements of a better order, with two rooms on each floor, are let at rents ranging from 3s. to 3s. 6d. In numerous instances these last houses are occupied by power-loom ribbon weavers and the hands employed in the foundries. The ground-floors are almost invariably paved with brick. Courts are very common in the crowded parts of the town." [Morning Chronicle 1850.] The church of All Saints is a principal ornament to D. It was formerly collegiate, and had a master and 7 prebendaries. It has a tower of peculiar beauty, in the latest style of pointed architecture. To this tower has been added a handsome modern church of classical architecture, with embellishments of the Roman-Doric order. The interior is light, elegant, and spacious, and contains splendid monuments of the Devonshire, Shrewsbury, and Cavendish families. There are in D. 8 churches of the establishment, a Unitarian chapel, a Roman Catholic chapel, a Friends meeting-house, 2 Congregational chapels, 4 Baptist, 3 Wesleyan, 2 Primitive Methodist, and I Swedenborgian chapel.—The Sunday schools in the town were attended in 1849 by 5,735 children; the national and infant schools by 1,600. The grammar-school of D., though not highly endowed, is supposed to be one of the most ancient endowments in England. It originally belonged to D. abbey; but was granted to the corporation, in 1554, by Queen Mary. The total revenue derived from charities connected with this borough, in 1834, was nearly £2,000 per annum.—Poor rates in 1837, £5,369; in 1847, £4,254. A work-house has been erected here by the poor-law commissioners for the union of Derby, capable of accommodating 350 persons. The Derby poor-law union comprehends 7 parishes, embracing an area of 4 sq. m.; with a pop. returned in 1831 at 25,484; in 1841 at 35,015. Expenditure in 1847, £4,254. The area of the borough of D. is 1,660 acres. Pop. in 1801, 10,832; in 1831, 23,607; in 1841, 32,741; in 1851, 40,615.—The average mortality is 1 in 40. D. is a borough by prescription. It returned 2 members to parliament, previous to the passing of the reform act, a privilege which it still enjoys. The boundaries of the borough, which comprehended the ps. of All Saints, St. Werburgh, and St. Michael, with portions of the ps. of St. Peter and St. Alkmund, remain unaltered. The number of electors registered in 1832 under the reform act, was 1,384; in 1837, 1,731, of whom 474 were registered as freemen; in 1846, 2,177.—D. is one of the polling-places, and the chief place of election, for the S division of Derbyshire. It possesses a commission-of-the-peace, and court-of-quarter-sessions. The municipal act of 1835 gave the borough a magistracy consisting of a mayor, 12 aldermen, and 36 councillors, dividing it, at the same time, into 6 wards. The income of the borough, in 1839, was £5,928 4s. 7d.; expenditure, £5,733 16s. 1d. In 1846-7 the revenue was £9,760; the expenditure, £8,102. The town is watched by night. Formerly 20 watchmen were employed in this duty, but 10 became sufficient when the watch-clock—an instrument first used by the late William Strutt, Esq., in his extensive manufactories -was applied to the service of the D. public. This simple but ingenious invention, consists in a number of clocks stationed in various quarters of the town, which require the repeated presence of the watchmen at fixed periods of the night, to prevent an inaccurate registration of their times of absence. The clocks are all kept locked; and each watchman has his own key, which only opens one check-clock. Water is supplied by a company to the lower parts of the town, by pumping from the Derwent, for which purpose new machinery and works have recently been constructed. The higher parts are supplied by springs and wells. The assizes for the co. are held here; the April sessions are held at Chesterfield, but the others in D. The county jail was erected here in 1824-6, at an expense of £63,335; the shire-hall and countycourt in 1828-32, at an expense of £18,502. The county jail is in an open and airy situation, in the outskirts of the town. It is enclosed with a brickwall 25 feet in height, flanked with towers loopholed for musketry. The interior of the prison consists of a central building—the chapel and keeper's house-with 7 radiating wings of two stories each, connected with it by iron bridges. The gaol comprises 22 wards, 22 day-rooms, 167 cells, and 20 airing-yards.
